TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' presents a bounded transition (the action of being impaled) as a completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect definition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'who has been impaled' asserts a realized fact in the actual world within the angel's knowledge, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' contains progressive marking ('been' + past participle) in addition to the present perfect auxiliary 'has', which instantiates a tense/mood specification beyond the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been impaled' exhibits overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'impaled'), with the semantic patient 'Jesus' functioning as the grammatical subject and the agent omitted.

FullText: » He is not here , for he has risen just as he promised . Come ; see the place where the Lord lay .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen' is an intransitive verb with no overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it describes a one-participant event where the subject undergoes a change of state rather than directing an action toward an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' encodes a one-participant change-of-state event (rising/resurrection) where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme), with no external argument (agent) licensed or introduced.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (he) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the change, not as an external argument (agent/initiator).
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a punctual boundary transition (change from a state of being dead/laid to a state of being alive/risen) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for instantaneous change-of-state events.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (from dead to alive/resurrected), crossing a definable threshold at a specific temporal boundary, regardless of any preceding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of a defined result state (transition from dead to alive), with an inherent endpoint, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from dead to alive/risen), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change-of-state event (from a state of being dead/laid to a state of being risen) involving a bounded dynamic transition, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'has risen' as an actual event that occurred in the world, with no indication of counterfactuality; it is a factual statement about a past event, not a contrary-to-fact situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'not' in the sentence negates 'is here' (the matrix clause), not the target gram 'has risen', which expresses a positive eventuality that did occur.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has risen' entails a change of location (from the tomb to risen state) with a resulting state that holds at the reference time—the subject is no longer in the place where he lay, which is the relevant current state for the discourse.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has risen' encodes a change of state (from dead to alive/risen) with the resulting state persisting at the reference time, as evidenced by the explanation 'He is not here' and the invitation to 'see the place where the Lord lay'—indicating the risen state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has risen' describes a discrete bounded event (the resurrection) with focus on the transition into a new state rather than a continuous situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' denotes a transition event (from dead to alive) construed as a single completed bounded whole, presenting the transition itself as a discrete event without profiling internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'he has risen' asserts a realized fact in the actual world as the reason for his absence, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'risen' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen' is active voice where the grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent (the one performing the action of rising); it lacks the passive morphology of 'have been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » Go quickly and tell his disciples he has risen from the dead . See , he goes before you to Galilee . You will see him there , as I have told you . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); the prepositional phrase 'from the dead' is an adjunct indicating source/origin, not an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' encodes a one-participant change-of-state event (from dead to alive) where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme), with no external argument (agent) licensed or introduced.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location/change-of-state event where the sole argument (he) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient undergoing the change) rather than an external argument (agent/initiator), matching the set_to_0_if criterion for unaccusative verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual boundary transition (from dead to alive state) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for instantaneous change-of-state events.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has risen from the dead' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (crossing the threshold from death to life), instantaneous in conception despite any preceding causality, matching the achievement prototype of definable threshold crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails a specific, inherent endpoint and defined result state (transition from dead to risen), marking a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen from the dead' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from dead to alive), which violates the core requirement that semelfactives must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change-of-state event (transition from dead to alive) involving a bounded dynamic event with an inherent endpoint, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (he has risen), without any indication that it is contrary to fact; it is a factual statement about a completed event, not a counterfactual condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has risen' expresses a positive eventuality with no negator directly negating it; the sentence presents the rising as occurring, not as absent from reality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has risen from the dead' entails a change of state (transition from dead to alive) whose resulting state holds at the reference time, and the subsequent clause 'he goes before you to Galilee' confirms this state is relevant for ongoing discourse.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has risen' encodes a transition from a state of being dead to a state of being alive, with the resulting state (resurrection/aliveness) persisting at the reference time, as evidenced by the subsequent clause 'he goes before you to Galilee' indicating current agency.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has risen' describes a discrete, bounded transition event (moving from death to life) rather than a continuous state or activity holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' denotes a transition event (from dead to alive) construed as a completed, bounded whole without internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect definition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'he has risen from the dead' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world within the narrative context, not projected as hypothetical, conditional, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'risen' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent (the one performing the action of rising), and the construction lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); this is an active intransitive verb in present perfect tense.

FullText: » Go quickly and tell his disciples he has risen from the dead . See , he goes before you to Galilee . You will see him there , as I have told you . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have told' introduces an overt internal argument 'you' (direct object), instantiating a two-participant event directed from the external argument toward an affected internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'told' licenses an external argument (the speaker/agent) and introduces an overt indirect object 'you', violating both conditions in set_to_0_if.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'told' licenses an internal argument (indirect object 'you'), violating the requirement for zero internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a punctual speech act of communication that is completed at a single moment without a durative process phase; it lacks the inherent temporal unfolding toward a defined endpoint characteristic of accomplishments.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have told' denotes a punctual transition into a state of having communicated information, representing a completed speech act that crosses a definable threshold from non-told to told, regardless of any preparatory speaking process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness or communication of information with a defined result state (the hearer now knows what was told), not a dynamic durative activity without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have told' entails a resulting state (the disciples now possess the information that was communicated) and describes a completed action with consequent result, not a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have told' denotes a bounded perceptual/communicative event marking the completion of a past act of telling, which involves dynamic change and eventive progression rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'as I have told you' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the narrative world (a prior telling), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have told' is not negated; the sentence is positive and the eventuality of telling is presented as realized, not absent.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have told' entails a change in the subject's informational state (the disciples now possess knowledge), and this resulting state of the disciples being informed remains relevant at the reference time for understanding why they should believe the prediction about seeing him in Galilee.
DtRes_explanation: The communication verb 'have told' merely reports a past interaction (prior instruction) without entailing that the information is currently binding or in effect; the clause does not encode a persisting resulting state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'as I have told you' describes a discrete past communicative event (the act of telling) rather than a continuous state or activity holding from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have told' presents the past act of telling as a bounded, completed event with relevance to the present moment, construing the communication as a discrete whole rather than profiling its internal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have told' asserts a realized past action in the actual world (a prior communication that occurred), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.

FullText: Jesus came to them and spoke to them , saying : » All authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth .

AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'has been given' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (authority transferred to Jesus) at a definable boundary point, regardless of any preceding process, matching the achievement criterion of instantaneous state-change.

DtRes_explanation: The passive construction 'has been given' encodes a change of state (authority transferred to Jesus) with the resulting state (Jesus possessing authority) persisting at the reference time, satisfying the DtRes feature.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has been given' describes a transition into a new state (authority being transferred to Jesus) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative example 'I have eaten the rice.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive form 'has been given' presents a bounded transition (the act of authority being granted) as a completed whole event, matching the positive example 'Many have been made sinners.'

FullText: » Teach them to observe all the things I have commanded you . Behold , I am with you always , even to the end of the age . «

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' encodes a transition from a state of non-command to a state where commands are in effect; the resulting state (the commands being binding/currently in effect) persists at the reference time, as evidenced by the imperative 'Teach them to observe all the things I have commanded.'
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' focuses on a completed action of issuing commands (transition into a state of having given commands) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the clause describes discrete bounded events (the commands themselves) rather than continuous duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have commanded' presents the act of commanding as a bounded, completed transition event without profiling its internal temporal development, fitting the perfective aspect definition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'I have commanded you' asserts a realized action in the actual world as a completed fact, not as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.

VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (doer) of the command, and 'you' is the grammatical object functioning as the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no 'been' auxiliary and no passive morphology.

FullText: Jesus said : » The time has come . The kingdom of God is at hand . Repent and believe the good news . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it only has an external argument (the time) and describes a state change without affecting a patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' encodes a one-participant change-of-state event where the sole argument ('time') functions as an internal argument (theme), with no external argument licensed or introduced.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has come' encodes a transition from a state where the time had not arrived to a state where it has now arrived and holds at the reference time, entailing a resulting state that persists.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' describes a discrete bounded event (the arrival/transition of a specific moment in time) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for transitions into new states.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'come' denotes a transition event (the arrival/occurrence of time), and 'has come' presents this transition as a bounded, completed whole without profiling its internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'The time has come' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world as a fact, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish contexts applying.

FullText: » What do you want with us Jesus of Nazareth ? Have you come to destroy us ? I know who you are . You are the Holy One of God ! «

DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (arrival with purpose to destroy) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the infinitive 'to destroy' expresses a transition into a new state.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have come' presents the transition of arrival as a bounded, completed event without profiling internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect feature.

FullText: He got up , took the bed and went out before them all . They were all filled with wonder . They gave glory to God and said we have never seen anything like this .

TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'we have never seen anything like this' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world—a factual claim about past experience—despite its negative form, and is not embedded in a modal, conditional, future, or wish context.

FullText: Jesus heard this . He said to them : » Those who are well have no need of a medical man but those who are ill do . I have come to care for sinners not the upright . «

TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have come' presents the transition of arrival as a bounded, completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, fitting the perfective aspect definition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'I have come to care for sinners' asserts a realized fact in the actual world as a statement of purpose/identity by Jesus, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.

FullText: When his own people heard of this they went to apprehend him . They said : » He has lost his senses . «

FullText: » If Satan has risen up against himself , he is divided , cannot stand and will come to an end .

DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the doing of 'this thing') rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the focus is on the completed action itself, not on duration.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'who had done this thing' is a relative clause asserting a realized fact about the woman in the actual world, not projecting a hypothetical or non-actual situation.

FullText: He said : » Daughter your faith has made you whole . Go in peace and be healed of your affliction . «

AktAch_explanation: The event 'has made whole' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (wholeness/healing) rather than an unfolding process, representing the instantaneous boundary point where the subject crosses from afflicted to healed.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has made whole' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from afflicted to healed), which violates the AktSemel feature requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' denotes a change-of-state event (transformation from afflicted to healed) with a clear endpoint, involving dynamic progression and internal eventive unfolding, which is incompatible with AktState.

DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' describes a punctual transition into a new state (from afflicted to healed) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative example 'I have broken the vase.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has made whole' presents a bounded transition (faith causing a change of state to wholeness) as a completed event, construed as a single accomplished fact rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has made you whole' is asserted as a realized fact within the speaker's utterance, describing an actual state of affairs that has occurred in the world of the narrative.

FullText: John the Baptist had become well known . King Herod heard that John had risen from the dead . Therefore he was doing these powerful works .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'become' encodes a one-participant change-of-state event where the sole argument 'John the Baptist' functions as an internal argument (theme), with no external argument (agent) licensed or introduced.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'become' denotes a change-of-state event where the sole argument 'John the Baptist' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the state change to 'well known', not as an external argument/agent.

AktAch_explanation: The event 'become well known' represents a punctual transition into a new result state (crossing the threshold from unknown to known), despite any preparatory period of gaining recognition.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had become well known' entails a transition into a resulting state (the state of being well known), which violates the core requirement that semelfactives must not entail a change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had become well known' denotes a change-of-state event (transition from unknown to known) with an inherent endpoint, involving dynamic progression rather than a static condition that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'had become well known' as a factual event that actually occurred in the narrative world, using past perfect for temporal anteriority within the narrative, without any counterfactual marking or conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had become well known' is not negated; the sentence affirms that John the Baptist had become well known, with no negator present to negate this eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'become well known' entails a change of state (from unknown to known) where the resulting state of being well known holds at the reference time, satisfying the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had become well known' encodes a transition from an unknown state to a known state, with the resulting state (being well known) persisting at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had become well known' describes a transition into a new state (becoming known) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion about transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had become well known' presents the transition into the state of being well-known as a bounded, completed event, construing the transition itself as a single whole rather than its internal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had become well known' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world as a past fact, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had become well known' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes past tense specification other than simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'had become well known' uses 'become' (a copular verb) with an adjectival complement 'well known', not the passive auxiliary sequence 'have + been + past participle'; this is a copular construction, not passive voice morphology.

FullText: John the Baptist had become well known . King Herod heard that John had risen from the dead . Therefore he was doing these powerful works .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'risen' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it only takes a prepositional phrase 'from the dead' which is not an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' encodes a one-participant change-of-state event (coming back to life) where the sole argument 'he' (John) functions as an internal argument (theme), with no external argument (agent) licensed or introduced.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument (John) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the change, not as an external argument/agent, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for unaccusative verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'risen from the dead' denotes a punctual boundary transition (crossing from dead to alive state) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for instantaneous change-of-state events.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'risen from the dead' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (moving from dead to alive), representing a definable threshold crossing regardless of any preceding process, consistent with achievement semantics.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'risen from the dead' entails a transition into a new resulting state (being alive after being dead), which violates the core requirement that semelfactive events must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The phrase 'had risen from the dead' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (resurrection) with a clear endpoint and internal eventive unfolding, which violates the stative requirement of holding over time without dynamic change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a situation that King Herod believed had actually occurred (John risen from the dead), not a counterfactual condition; it is embedded in indirect discourse ('heard that') reporting what Herod thought to be true in his world, not expressing an unfulfilled or contrary-to-fact situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had risen from the dead' is not negated; King Herod heard that this event occurred, and the sentence presents it as a realized state without negation.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'rise from the dead' entails a permanent change of state (transition from dead to alive) that necessarily holds at the reference time, and the resulting state is narratively relevant for explaining Herod's subsequent actions ('he was doing these powerful works').
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had risen from the dead' encodes a transition from a state of being dead to a state of being alive, with the resulting state (being alive) persisting at the reference time of King Herod's perception and subsequent actions.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had risen from the dead' describes a discrete bounded event (a transition from death to life) rather than a continuous state or activity holding over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion about transitions into new states.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'risen from the dead' denotes a transition event (moving from dead to alive) construed as a bounded, completed whole without internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'had risen from the dead' is embedded within King Herod's belief or rumor ('heard that'), projecting a hypothetical or non-actual situation rather than asserting it as a realized fact in the actual world.

FullText: The apostles gathered together with Jesus . They told him all things they had done and taught .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (the apostles as agents performing actions), violating the requirement for a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'all things'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realizes an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes completed actions without inherent durative process or defined terminal point; it is a punctual achievement marking the boundary of action completion rather than an accomplishment with measurable duration toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had done' refers to a completed durative process or series of activities (missionary work, preaching, healing) that unfolded over measurable duration rather than a punctual state-change boundary, and it naturally functions as the complement of an implicit 'finish' (they finished doing these things).
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'had done' refers to completed actions (things they did and taught) with an inherent endpoint and defined result state, not a dynamic durative situation without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had done' refers to a completed series of actions (missionary work) that unfolded over a measurable duration rather than a single punctual occurrence, and the context construes it as a durative activity without the instantaneous, bounded quality required for semelfactive marking.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had done' is morphologically marked as past perfect progressive aspect (had + done), which entails dynamic eventive unfolding and change, incompatible with stative readings.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had done' expresses a realized event (the apostles performed actions) with no negator present in the sentence to negate this eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'they had done and taught' presents actions as part of the apostles' life history without asserting that a resulting state currently holds; it answers what they had accomplished, fitting the experiential perfect.
DtRes_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' encodes a transition to a completed state (the apostles' actions were finished) that held at the reference time (when they told Jesus), satisfying the resultative feature.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (things they had done and taught) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for 'discrete bounded events.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' presents the apostles' actions as bounded, completed events without internal temporal development, fitting the perfective aspect definition of a situation construed as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'they had done' asserts a realized fact in the actual world—the apostles actually performed actions that they reported to Jesus, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.

FullText: They had not understood the loaves of bread . Their minds were closed .

DtExp_explanation: The negative perfect 'had not understood' presents a non-occurrence as part of the subjects' life history without asserting a resulting state that persists at reference time, matching the pattern of negative perfects in the positive examples.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is negated ('had not understood'), so no transition to a resulting state is entailed; the rule 'The clause negates the occurrence of the event; no transition to a resulting state is entailed' applies.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a state of non-understanding (a negative state) at a past reference point rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the state itself rather than duration across a time span.

FullText: Then he replied : » Thanks to your answer you may leave for the demon has gone out of your daughter . «

DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has gone out of' describes a discrete bounded event (the demon's departure/transition out of the daughter) rather than a continuous state or activity holding over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion about transitions into new states.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has gone out of' denotes a transition event (the demon departing from the daughter) construed as a completed bounded whole, matching the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'the demon has gone out of your daughter' asserts a realized fact in the actual world as the reason justifying the permission to leave, not a projected or hypothetical situation.

FullText: They came down the mountain . He warned them not to tell anyone what they had seen until after the Son of man had risen from the dead .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'had seen' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'they' as agent/experiencer) and takes a direct object ('what'), violating both the external argument and overt object conditions for ASUncc.

AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a punctual perceptual event—the instantaneous transition into visual awareness—without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement category.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (instantaneous discovery of a visual experience) rather than an unfolding durative process, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news.'
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness of a specific visual stimulus, which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had seen' entails a resulting state (knowledge of what was witnessed) and does not construe as a single punctual occurrence but rather as a completed durative experience with consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness (seeing something), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the seeing occurred before the warning), not to indicate a counterfactual situation; the event actually occurred in the story world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in the sentence applies to 'tell', not to the target gram 'had seen'; the warning is against telling, not against the seeing itself.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' presents the event of seeing as part of the disciples' life history without asserting that a resulting state currently holds; the focus is on the occurrence of the perceptual event itself, not on a persistent consequence.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had seen' is embedded in a temporal clause ('until after the Son of man had risen') and reports a past perceptual event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it merely describes what they witnessed, not a change of state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had seen' describes a discrete bounded event (witnessing something specific) rather than a continuous state or activity holding over time, and the focus is on the completed action itself, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had seen' presents the visual perception event as a bounded, completed whole prior to the reference time of the warning, construing it as a discrete transition into the state of having witnessed something.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' asserts a realized event in the actual world (they did see something), embedded within a reported speech context that does not project or hypothesize the seeing itself.

FullText: They came down the mountain . He warned them not to tell anyone what they had seen until after the Son of man had risen from the dead .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' encodes a one-participant change-of-state event (from dead to alive) where the sole argument (Son of man) functions as an internal argument/theme with no external agent licensing, matching the intuition for ASUncc.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had risen from the dead' entails a transition into a new resulting state (being alive/resurrected), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had risen from the dead' denotes a change-of-state event (transition from dead to alive) with a clear boundary and dynamic progression, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the Son of man rising after the warning is given), not to indicate a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.

DtRes_explanation: The clause 'had risen from the dead' encodes a transition from a state of being dead to a state of being alive, with the resulting state (being alive/risen) persisting at the reference time of the conditional temporal clause.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had risen from the dead' describes a discrete bounded event (a transition into a new state) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the transition itself.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had risen from the dead' presents a transition event (rising from death) as a bounded, completed whole without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'had risen from the dead' is embedded within a temporal condition ('until after') that projects a future event relative to the warning, construing it as hypothetical and non-actual at the time of speaking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had risen' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes tense specification other than simple present perfect, meeting the TLTS feature criteria.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'had risen from the dead' is an active voice past perfect with an intransitive verb; it lacks the passive morphology (auxiliary 'been' + past participle) required for VcPass, and the grammatical subject 'Son of man' is the semantic agent/undergoer of the action, not a patient in a passive construction.

FullText: » But I say to you that Elijah has come . They have done to him what ever they wished just as it is written about him . «

DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'They have done to him what ever they wished' describes discrete bounded events (specific actions performed on Elijah) rather than a continuous situation holding over time.

FullText: They said nothing because they had had an argument between themselves about who was the greatest .

DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has joined together' describes a discrete bounded event (the action of joining) resulting in a new state, with focus on the transition/completion of that action rather than a situation holding continuously over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has joined together' presents a bounded transition (the joining of two entities into a unified whole) as a completed event, construed as a single accomplished fact rather than an ongoing process or unbounded state.
