FullText: » But to sit on my right hand or on my left hand is not mine to give . It is for them for whom it has been prepared . «
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'has been prepared' introduces an overt internal argument ('it', referring to the right/left hand), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant event with an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been prepared'), and the lexical verb 'prepare' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent who prepared it), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'prepared' licenses an internal argument (the implicit object 'it'/the seats), and the sole expressed argument functions as a theme/patient undergoing preparation rather than as an external agent.
AktAcc_explanation: The passive construction 'has been prepared' denotes a durative process of preparation that necessarily culminates in a defined result state (the thing being ready/prepared), fitting the accomplishment profile of AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has been prepared' unfolds over a measurable duration as a process of making something ready, rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and can naturally be conceived as the completion of a durative preparatory process.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'has been prepared' denotes a bounded event with an inherent endpoint and defined result state (something is now in a prepared state), not a dynamic durative activity without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been prepared' entails a resulting change of state (something transitioning from unprepared to prepared), which violates the core requirement that semelfactives must not entail a resulting state change.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been prepared' is morphologically marked with progressive perfect aspect ('has been'), which entails dynamic unfolding and change-of-state (preparation is a process of making something ready), incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual situation (something has been prepared for certain people) without any indication that it is contrary to fact; it describes an actual state of affairs, not a counterfactual condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'not' in the sentence negates 'is mine to give' (the matrix clause), not the target gram 'has been prepared' in the relative clause, which presents the preparation as a realized event.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has been prepared' entails a change of state (the seats being prepared/made ready), and that resulting state of preparedness holds at the reference time, making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The passive construction 'has been prepared' encodes a transition from an unprepared to a prepared state, with the resulting state (readiness for those whom it is intended) persisting at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a completed preparation (transition into a prepared state) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative example 'I have eaten the rice (and now it is gone).'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive form 'has been prepared' presents a bounded transition (the preparation event) as a completed whole without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has been prepared' is embedded within a relative clause describing a future or predetermined state ('for whom it has been prepared'), projecting a non-actual or hypothetical allocation rather than asserting a realized fact in the actual world.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been prepared' contains progressive/passive marking ('been' + past participle 'prepared') beyond the canonical present perfect form, instantiating TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has been prepared' contains the passive morphology (auxiliary 'has been' + past participle 'prepared'), the grammatical subject 'it' functions as the semantic patient (the thing being prepared), and the agent is omitted, satisfying all criteria for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus told him : » Go your way ; your faith has made you whole . And immediately he received his sight , and followed him in the way . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has made' introduces an overt internal argument 'you' (direct object), encoding a two-participant event where faith (external argument) is construed as directed toward and affecting the patient 'you'.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an external argument (your faith as agent) and introduces an overt direct object (you/whole), violating both conditions in set_to_0_if.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'made whole' licenses an internal argument ('you'), violating the requirement for a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' denotes a punctual change-of-state (transition from broken/sick to whole/healed) without a durative process phase, matching the pattern of achievement verbs like 'break' and 'arrive' in the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has made you whole' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (from illness/brokenness to wholeness), construed as an instantaneous boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process, consistent with the achievement examples 'found the key' and 'reached the summit'.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails a specific, inherent endpoint and defined result state (wholeness/healing achieved), and the verb denotes a bounded perceptual or cognitive event marking the onset of awareness/completion of a transformative act, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has made whole' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the person becomes whole/healed), which violates the core requirement that semelfactive events must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has made whole' denotes a change-of-state event (transition from illness/brokenness to wholeness) with a clear endpoint, involving dynamic progression incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (the man's faith has made him whole and he immediately received his sight), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has made whole' is a positive assertion of an event that occurred; there is no negator present that negates this eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' entails a change of state (from sick/broken to whole/healed) and that resulting state holds at the reference time, as evidenced by the immediate consequence 'he received his sight.'
DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'has made you whole' encodes a transition from a state of illness/incompleteness to wholeness, with the resulting state (being whole/healed) persisting at the reference time, as evidenced by the immediate consequence 'he received his sight.'
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has made whole' describes a transition into a new state (from being sick/broken to being whole) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative example 'I have eaten the rice (and now it is gone).'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has made whole' presents a bounded transition (the action of making whole) as a completed event, with the result state accessible at the reference time, matching the perfective aspect profile.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has made you whole' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world as a direct statement of fact by Jesus, not as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has made' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'made' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'your faith' is the semantic agent (doer) of the verb 'made', and 'you' is the grammatical object functioning as the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no auxiliary 'been' + past participle morphology.

FullText: go into the village that is nearby . » As soon as you enter it you will find a colt tied there . No man has ever sat on that colt . Untie him and bring him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'sat on' introduces an overt internal argument ('that colt'), making it a transitive construction with an affected patient, thus instantiating ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'sat on' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'man' or 'no one'), making it a two-participant event rather than a one-participant internal-argument-only event.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'sat on' licenses an internal argument (the colt as the object of the preposition 'on'), violating the requirement for zero internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'sat on' denotes a punctual change-of-state (transition into the seated position) without a durative process phase; the event is a boundary transition rather than an unfolding process culminating in a defined result state.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'sat on' unfolds over a measurable duration (the act of sitting involves an extended temporal phase) rather than occurring at a single punctual boundary transition, and it can naturally occur as a complement of 'finish' (e.g., 'finish sitting on'), indicating a durative process.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'has sat on' entails a specific result state (being in a seated position on the colt) and can be modified by 'in X time' (e.g., 'sat on in five minutes'), indicating a bounded event with an inherent endpoint rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'sat on' entails a resulting state (being in a seated position on the colt) and is construed as a durative activity rather than a single punctual occurrence, failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has sat on' is morphologically marked with present perfect progressive aspect ('has...sat') denoting a bounded perceptual/experiential event (the act of sitting on the colt), which involves dynamic change and eventive unfolding incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever sat on that colt' presents a factual statement about the actual world (the colt has not been ridden), not a situation contrary to fact under different conditions.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'No' directly negates the eventuality expressed by the target gram 'has sat on', presenting the event as not occurring.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'has ever sat on' with the frequency adverbial 'ever' presents the event as part of the colt's life history (whether anyone has ever had the experience of sitting on it), without asserting that a resulting state currently holds.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever sat on that colt' reports a past non-occurrence (negation) with a frequency adverbial ('ever'), indicating no resulting state persists at the reference time; it merely describes the colt's experiential history without entailing a current change of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a negative state (no one has ever sat on the colt), which focuses on the absence of discrete events rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the adverbial 'ever' emphasizes the non-occurrence of bounded events.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever sat on that colt' is a stative situation (a negative existential state) without a construed boundary—it describes an unbounded condition of non-occurrence rather than presenting a bounded transition as a completed whole event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has sat on' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (no man has ever sat on the colt is a factual claim about what has not occurred), and negation alone does not trigger irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has sat on' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'sat', with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'No man' corresponds to the semantic agent (the one doing the sitting), and 'that colt' is the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, as the agent is the subject and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle).

FullText: When he taught , he said : » Is it not written , my house shall be called a house of prayer for all the nations ? But you have made it a den of robbers . «
Target: have made (it a den of robbers)
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have made' introduces an overt internal argument 'it' (direct object) and a predicative complement 'a den of robbers', encoding a two-participant event where the external argument (you) affects the internal argument (it).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have made' licenses an external argument (the agent 'you') and introduces an overt direct object ('it'), violating both conditions in set_to_0_if.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an internal argument ('it') and a predicative complement ('a den of robbers'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realizes an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have made it a den of robbers' denotes a durative process of transformation (converting the house from a prayer house to a den of robbers) that culminates in a defined result state (it being a den of robbers), satisfying the AktAcc feature.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have made it a den of robbers' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (the house becoming a den of robbers), representing a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails a specific result state (transforming the house into a den of robbers) and can be modified by 'in X time' (you have made it a den of robbers in three years), indicating a bounded event with an inherent endpoint rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have made it a den of robbers' entails a transition into a resulting state (the house becoming a den of robbers), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have made' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving change-of-state (transforming the house from a house of prayer into a den of robbers), which entails internal eventive unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents an actual occurrence in the world ('you have made it a den of robbers') as a factual statement of what has happened, without any indication of counterfactuality or contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have made' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('Is it not written') applies to a different clause and does not negate the eventuality of making the house a den of robbers.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have made it a den of robbers' entails a change of state (the house's status/function) that results in a current state of affairs at the reference time, which meets the set_to_0_if criterion.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have made' encodes a transition from a prior state (house as prayer place) to a resulting state (den of robbers) that persists at the reference time, entailing a change of condition that currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have made' describes a discrete bounded event (the transformation of the house into a den of robbers) rather than a continuous state or activity holding over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion about transitions into new states.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have made' presents a bounded transition (the action of converting the house into a den of robbers) as a completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, satisfying the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'you have made it a den of robbers' asserts a realized action in the actual world as a factual accusation, not as hypothetical, conditional, or otherwise non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have made' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'made', with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'you' is the semantic agent (doer) of the verb 'made', and 'it' is the grammatical object functioning as the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no auxiliary 'been' + past participle passive morphology.

FullText: » Have you not read this Scripture : ‘ The stone the builders rejected was made the chief corner stone . ’
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'read' introduces an overt internal argument ('this Scripture'), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant event with an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'read' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you') and introduces an overt direct object ('this Scripture'), violating both conditions for ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'read' licenses an internal argument ('this Scripture'), making it transitive and thus not instantiating ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'read' denotes a durative process of consuming text that culminates in a defined endpoint (completion of reading), fitting the accomplishment profile; the perfect aspect 'have not read' presupposes this completed or resultant state.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'read' unfolds over a measurable duration as a process of comprehending text, rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and can naturally be completed ('finish reading'), both of which set it to 0 per the CRIs.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have not read' with the internal argument 'this Scripture' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (reading and comprehending a specific text), which constitutes a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'read' entails a transition into a resulting state of knowledge/comprehension and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing both criteria for semelfactive classification.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have not read' is morphologically marked as perfect aspect (present perfect), which encodes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the completion of the reading action, thus involving dynamic change and eventive unfolding incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual question about whether the addressee has actually read Scripture in the actual world, without any indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have not read' directly instantiates ClNegPol because the negator 'not' syntactically negates the eventuality of reading, presenting the event as not occurring.
DtExp_explanation: The negative perfect 'have not read' presents the non-occurrence of reading as part of the addressee's life history, answering whether they have ever encountered this Scripture, without asserting any resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is negated ('have not read'), so no transition to a resulting state is entailed; the negation blocks the resultative reading.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (reading a specific Scripture passage) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the completion/non-completion of that bounded action.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have not read' is negated with 'not', which indicates the transition (reading) has not occurred at the reference time, leaving the situation open rather than presenting it as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The interrogative form 'Have you not read' projects a hypothetical or uncertain state rather than asserting a realized fact; it seeks confirmation of whether the addressee has engaged with the Scripture, treating the situation as not yet established in the actual world.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have not read' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have not read' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'you' as the semantic agent (doer) and the object 'this Scripture' as the semantic patient; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle, which is absent here.

FullText: » The tribulation in these days will be greater then there has ever been from the beginning of God’s creation until now , and never shall be .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'be' functions as a copula linking the subject 'tribulation' to the predicative complement 'greater', not introducing an internal argument, and thus does not instantiate ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The copular verb 'be' with 'tribulation' as the sole internal argument (theme) encodes a one-participant state without licensing an external argument, matching the positive example of copular 'be' clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'been' (copula with 'tribulation' as subject) denotes existence/state rather than a one-participant event with an external argument; 'tribulation' functions as the internal argument (theme) undergoing the state of existence, not as an agent/initiator.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has been' denotes a stative condition (existence of tribulation) rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent endpoint; it expresses a punctual or habitual state without a process phase leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'has (ever) been [tribulation]' denotes a stative condition of existence rather than a punctual change-of-state transition, and the comparative construction 'greater than there has ever been' treats the state as durative and measurable rather than as a boundary point.
AktAct_explanation: The situation denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness ('has been [tribulation]' = awareness of tribulation's existence), which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has (ever) been [tribulation]' expresses a state of existence over an extended temporal span (from creation until now) rather than a single punctual, instantaneous occurrence, thus it does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has ever been [tribulation]' is marked with perfect progressive aspect ('has been'), which entails dynamic unfolding and eventive change over time, incompatible with stative readings per CRI set_to_0_if rule 2.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual statement about what has actually occurred in the past ('there has ever been [tribulation]'), describing a real situation without any indication of counterfactuality or unfulfilled conditions.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has (ever) been [tribulation]' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('never shall be') applies to the future state, not to the past eventuality expressed by the target gram.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'been [tribulation]' entails a state of existence/occurrence, and the comparative structure 'greater than there has ever been' focuses on the resulting state of tribulation existing at reference time rather than merely recording an occurrence in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: This is a stative copula construction ('has been [adjective]') that describes a state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; it merely reports a past condition without a persisting change of state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'has ever been' with the temporal span 'from the beginning of God's creation until now' expresses a state (tribulation existing) that has held continuously across the entire time period from creation to the reference time, matching the intuition for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'been [tribulation]' denotes a stative situation (existence/presence of tribulation) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not introduce a transition into or out of that state, making it unbounded rather than perfective.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has ever been' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (that tribulation has existed from creation until now), functioning as a comparative baseline for the main clause, not as a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has ever been' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by a past participle 'been' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the noun phrase 'tribulation' (a non-verbal predicate), making 'been' a lexical copula rather than a passive auxiliary, so this is not passive voice morphology.

FullText: » Be alert for I have told you all things beforehand .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have told' introduces an overt internal argument ('you') and an overt affected theme ('all things'), satisfying the two-participant transitive event structure with an external argument (I) directing the action toward internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'told' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') and introduces an overt direct object ('you all things'), violating both conditions in set_to_0_if.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'told' licenses an internal argument ('you' as indirect object and 'all things' as direct object), violating the requirement for zero internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'told' denotes a punctual speech act that is instantaneous or near-instantaneous in execution, lacking a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, thus instantiating achievement rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have told' denotes a punctual transition into a result state (the hearer now possesses the information), representing a boundary point of communication rather than an unfolding process, consistent with achievement semantics.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails a specific, inherent endpoint (completion of telling all things) and a defined result state (having communicated information), making it a bounded accomplishment rather than a dynamic activity without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have told' entails a resulting state (the addressee now possesses knowledge of all things), which violates the core requirement that semelfactives must not entail a change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a completed communicative act (change-of-state from not-informed to informed), which involves dynamic eventive unfolding and a bounded endpoint, incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual statement of something that has actually occurred ('I have told you'), without any indication of counterfactuality or contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have told' is not negated; the negation 'not' in the sentence applies to 'Be alert' (the imperative), not to the eventuality of telling.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have told' presents the act of telling as a completed event in the subject's life history without asserting that a resulting state (such as the listener's knowledge) currently holds; the focus is on the occurrence of the telling event itself, not on its present consequences.
DtRes_explanation: The communication verb 'have told' entails that the information (being alert for things) is currently in effect as a directive binding on the addressee at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have told' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of telling) completed in the past rather than a continuous state or activity holding over time, and the focus is on the accomplishment of the telling action itself.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have told' presents the act of telling as a completed, bounded transition event without profiling its internal temporal development, meeting the core criterion for TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have told' asserts a realized action in the actual world (past perfect indicating a completed action), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have told' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'told' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have told' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent (doer) and the object 'you' as the semantic patient; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle, which is absent here.

FullText: » This ointment might have been sold for more than three hundred denarii . And the money could have been given to the poor . They scolded her . «
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'might have been sold' introduces an overt internal argument ('this ointment') that is affected by the selling event, satisfying the two-participant transitive requirement.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive (been sold), and the lexical verb 'sell' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent performing the sale), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'sold' is in passive voice with 'ointment' as the internal argument (theme/patient), not an external argument, and licenses an implicit agent, violating the one-participant external-argument-only requirement.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'sold' denotes a transaction that unfolds through a durative process (negotiation, exchange of goods and payment) culminating in a defined result state (ownership transfer), fitting the accomplishment profile despite the modal and passive framing.
AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'might have been sold' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (from unsold to sold), representing a definable threshold crossing regardless of the preparatory process of selling.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the completion of a sale transaction) and a defined result state (the ointment being sold), making it a bounded event rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'sold' entails a transition into a resulting state (change of ownership/possession), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'might have been sold' denotes a bounded dynamic event (change of ownership/state through the action of selling), which involves eventive unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a counterfactual situation using 'might have been sold,' expressing an unrealized possibility contrary to what actually occurred (the ointment was not sold), consistent with the apodosis pattern in counterfactual conditionals.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'might have been sold' expresses a possible event without negation; the modal 'might' indicates possibility rather than absence, and no negator directly negates this eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'sold' entails a change of possession (from seller to buyer) that would result in a state where the ointment is no longer possessed by the original owner, and this resulting state is relevant to the discourse context about the value of the ointment.
DtRes_explanation: The modal 'might' expresses possibility/counterfactuality rather than a completed transition; the clause does not entail that a resulting state (of being sold) actually holds at the reference time, only that it could have occurred.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (a hypothetical sale) rather than a continuous situation, and the explicit numerical bound 'three hundred denarii' indicates a specific transaction rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive construction 'might have been sold' presents a bounded transition event (the sale) as a completed whole without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The modal auxiliary 'might' combined with the perfect infinitive 'have been sold' construes the situation as speculative and non-actual rather than asserted as a realized fact in the actual world.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'might have been sold' contains modal marking ('might') combined with perfect aspect ('have' + past participle 'been sold'), which instantiates a tense/mood specification other than simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'might have been sold' instantiates VcPass: it contains the passive auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'sold', the semantic patient (ointment) functions as grammatical subject, and the agent is omitted.

FullText: » This ointment might have been sold for more than three hundred denarii . And the money could have been given to the poor . They scolded her . «
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'could have been given to the poor' contains an overt internal argument ('to the poor'), instantiating a two-participant event where the money is affected by the giving action.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive (been given), and the lexical verb 'give' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent who would give the money), violating the set_to_0_if condition.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'given' licenses an internal argument (the money as theme/patient receiving the action), and the sole argument functions as an internal argument rather than an external agent, violating the core requirement for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The passive construction 'could have been given' denotes a durative process of transferring money to the poor that culminates in a defined result state (money in possession of the poor), fitting the accomplishment profile despite the modal and conditional framing.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'could have been given' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (money transferred to recipients), representing a definable threshold moment rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'could have been given to the poor' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the completion of giving money) and a defined result state (money transferred to the poor), making it a bounded event rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'could have been given' entails a transition into a resulting state (money transferred to the poor), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'could have been given' denotes a bounded dynamic event (transfer of money) involving change-of-state and internal eventive unfolding, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'could have been given' is a counterfactual apodosis expressing an unrealized possibility contrary to what actually occurred—the money was not given to the poor, as indicated by the context of criticism.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'could have been given' expresses a possible event without negation; no negator applies to this gram, which describes a potential action rather than its absence.
DtExp_explanation: The modal perfect 'could have been given' presents a hypothetical event as a possible occurrence in the subject's life history without asserting that any resulting state currently holds; it expresses what might have happened rather than what did happen or what persists.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is counterfactual/conditional ('could have been given') and does not entail that a resulting state actually holds at the reference time; it expresses an unrealized possibility rather than a completed transition with a persisting result.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (giving money to the poor) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the modal 'could have' expresses a counterfactual possibility rather than an actual state or activity.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive construction 'could have been given' presents the transition of giving as a bounded, completed event (a passive bounded transition), matching the positive example 'We have been discharged.'
TLMdIrr_explanation: The modal auxiliary 'could' combined with 'have been given' construes the situation as hypothetical/conditional rather than asserted as realized fact in the actual world, instantiating irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'could have been given' contains modal marking ('could') combined with perfect aspect ('have' + past participle 'given'), which instantiates a tense/mood specification other than simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'could have been given' instantiates VcPass: it contains the passive auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'given', the grammatical subject 'money' is the semantic patient (recipient of the giving), and the agent is omitted.

FullText: » Truly I tell you everywhere the good news is preached through out the whole world , that which this woman has done will be spoken of for a memorial of her . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has done' introduces an overt internal argument ('that which this woman has done'), where the relative pronoun 'that which' functions as the direct object, encoding an affected patient/theme directed from the external argument (the woman) toward the internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has done' licenses an external argument (the woman as agent) and introduces an implicit internal argument (what she did), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the one-participant internal-argument-only criterion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has done' licenses an internal argument ('that which this woman has done'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realizes an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a punctual, completed action without an inherent durative process phase; it marks a boundary transition into a finished state rather than an event unfolding over measurable duration toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has done' denotes a punctual transition into a completed action with a definable result state (a memorial deed), construed as a boundary point rather than an unfolding process, matching the achievement prototype.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has done' refers to a completed action with a defined result state (the woman's deed), which will be memorialized; this entails a specific endpoint and bounded event rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has done' entails a resulting change of state (a memorial action with lasting consequences) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence without result, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has done' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (the woman's action) with an inherent endpoint, involving eventive change rather than a static condition that holds without progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world ('that which this woman has done'), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has done' is not negated; the sentence affirms that the woman's action will be spoken of, with no negator applying to the target gram itself.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'that which this woman has done will be spoken of for a memorial of her' presents the action as having a resulting state (being spoken of/remembered) that persists at the reference time, making it non-experiential rather than a simple life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'that which this woman has done will be spoken of for a memorial of her' reports a past action without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; the focus is on the future commemoration of the deed, not on a present state resulting from the action.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'this woman has done' describes a discrete bounded event (a specific action performed by the woman) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the completed action itself, not its duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has done' presents a completed action as a bounded whole without profiling internal temporal development, consistent with perfective aspect; the predicate denotes a discrete accomplishment event rather than a stative situation or progressive development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'that which this woman has done' asserts a realized action in the actual world as a completed fact, not as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'done' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'that which this woman' corresponds to the semantic agent (the woman is the doer), and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence); 'has done' is active voice.

FullText: » The Son of man goes even as it is written of him . Woe to that man through whom the Son of man is betrayed ! It would have been better if he had not been born . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'would have been better' functions as a copula construction linking the subject to a predicative complement ('better'), not introducing an overt internal argument affected by the verb.
ASUncc_explanation: The copular verb 'be' in 'would have been better' encodes a one-participant stative event where the sole argument (it, referring to the condition) functions as an internal argument, with no external argument licensed or introduced.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'be' in 'would have been better' denotes a state/quality rather than a one-participant event with an external argument; it is a copular construction where the subject functions as an internal argument (theme) receiving the predicate adjective, not as an agent/initiator.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative copular construction expressing a punctual evaluative judgment or state comparison, not a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase leading to a defined terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would have been better' expresses a stative condition or quality comparison rather than a dynamic change-of-state event with a punctual boundary transition; it describes a hypothetical state of affairs, not a transition into a new result state.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative predicate expressing a result state or condition, not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement; it lacks dynamic unfolding and does not instantiate an activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative predicate expressing a resulting state or condition, not a dynamic punctual event, and therefore does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' denotes a durative stative condition (a comparative attribute/state) with no dynamic change, internal unfolding, or eventive progression; it expresses a static evaluative relation rather than an event.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'would have been better' is the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional expressing an unfulfilled, contrary-to-fact situation: the person was born (actual fact), but the utterance presents an alternative scenario where non-birth would have been better (contrary to fact).
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have been better' is a positive comparative statement; the negation 'not' in the sentence applies to the embedded clause 'if he had not been born', not to the target gram itself.
DtExp_explanation: The construction 'would have been better' presents a counterfactual conditional expressing a resulting state of affairs (a better condition) rather than an event in the subject's life history, and the verb 'be' entails a state that would hold at the reference time.
DtRes_explanation: This is a stative copula construction ('would have been better') that describes a hypothetical condition rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target expresses a counterfactual conditional about a state transition (not being born), focusing on the resulting state rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and does not denote a continuous activity or habitual series from a past point to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'better' denotes a stative situation (a state of comparative quality) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not introduce a transition into or out of that state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' instantiates irrealis as it projects a counterfactual/hypothetical situation (what would be the case under a contrary-to-fact condition) rather than asserting a realized fact in the actual world.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' contains modal marking ('would') with the perfect form ('have been'), which instantiates a tense/mood specification other than simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adjective 'better' (a non-verbal predicate), making this a copular construction rather than a passive voice construction; passive voice requires 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: » The Son of man goes even as it is written of him . Woe to that man through whom the Son of man is betrayed ! It would have been better if he had not been born . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'be born' functions as a copula-like construction linking the subject to a predicative state without introducing an overt internal argument; the passive construction does not create an affected patient argument in the syntactic structure.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had not been born'), and the lexical verb 'born' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent who caused the birth), triggering the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses with external arguments.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'born' denotes a change-of-state/coming-into-being event where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) rather than an external argument, matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: Birth is a punctual boundary transition marking entry into existence, lacking a durative process phase within the event itself; the counterfactual negation 'had not been born' denotes a single instantaneous state-change rather than an accomplishment with measurable duration and defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had not been born' represents a punctual transition into the state of existence, a definable threshold crossing from non-existence to existence, despite any preparatory biological processes.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had not been born' denotes a single punctual event marking the onset of existence (or lack thereof) with a defined result state, not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'be born' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from non-existence to existence) and unfolds over a measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing both criteria for semelfactive classification.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' denotes a change-of-state event (birth) marking a bounded temporal boundary, involving dynamic progression from non-existence to existence, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had not been born' appears in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('if he had not been born') expressing an unfulfilled, contrary-to-fact condition about a person's existence.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had not been born' directly instantiates ClNegPol through the negator 'not', which negates the eventuality of being born, presenting the state as not occurring or not holding.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'be born' entails a change of state (from non-existence to existence) with a resulting state that persists at the reference time; the negation does not alter this—the subject's non-birth would be a permanent condition affecting their entire life history.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'he had not been born' is a stative copula construction (negated 'been') that describes a state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; it does not entail a change of state holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a counterfactual state of non-existence (not being born), which is a transition into a state rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the focus is on the absence of an event rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had not been born' is negated with 'not', indicating the transition has not occurred at the reference time, making the situation remain open rather than presenting it as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' is embedded within a counterfactual conditional construction ('It would have been better if he had not been born'), which projects a hypothetical, non-actual situation rather than asserting a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes past tense marking distinct from the simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not been born' instantiates VcPass: it contains the passive auxiliary sequence 'had been' + past participle 'born', the semantic patient (the subject 'he') functions as grammatical subject, and the agent is omitted, satisfying all criteria for passive voice morphology.

FullText: He took a cup , and when he had given praise , he gave it to them and they all had a drink from it .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had given' introduces an overt internal argument 'praise' (direct object), instantiating a two-participant event directed from the external argument toward an affected internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'give' in 'had given praise' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he') and introduces an overt direct object ('praise'), violating both conditions in set_to_0_if.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'give' licenses an internal argument ('praise' as direct object), violating the requirement for zero internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'give praise' denotes a punctual speech act or performative event that occurs at a single moment without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, similar to 'decide' or 'say'.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had given praise' represents a punctual transition into a new result state (the completion of the act of giving praise), construed as a boundary point rather than an unfolding durative process, meeting the achievement criterion.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the completion of giving praise) and is a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of a completed action, not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had given praise' entails a transition into a resulting state (praise has been given/completed) and does not represent a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had given praise' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving the completion of an action (giving praise), which constitutes change-of-state and eventive progression incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had given praise' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence (he gave praise before giving the cup), not to indicate a counterfactual situation that did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had given praise' is not negated; the sentence presents this event as occurring in reality within the temporal sequence of actions.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'when he had given praise' functions as a narrative progression event that sets the scene for the subsequent action 'he gave it to them'; the resulting state of having given praise is relevant for the discourse sequence, making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'when he had given praise' reports a past communicative act without entailing that the praise or its effects persist at the reference time; it merely sequences events in a narrative without a resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (giving praise) that occurs at a specific point in the narrative sequence, not a continuous situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had given praise' presents the act of giving praise as a bounded, completed transition event that occurred prior to the main clause action, without profiling its internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'when he had given praise' is embedded in a temporal sequence of realized events ('He took a cup...he gave it to them...they all had a drink'), asserting the situation as a completed fact in the actual world rather than as hypothetical, conditional, or otherwise non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had given praise' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes past tense specification other than simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of giving praise, and 'praise' is the grammatical object; this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no auxiliary 'been' + past participle construction.

FullText: He came a third time and said to them : » Sleep and take your rest . It is enough the hour has come . Look , the Son of man is betrayed into the hands of sinners !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' is an intransitive verb with no overt internal argument (direct or indirect object) in the clause 'the hour has come'; it does not introduce an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has come' encodes a one-participant change-of-location event where the sole argument (the hour) functions as an internal argument (theme), with no external argument licensed or introduced.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument ('the hour') functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing movement/change rather than as an external agent initiating the event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a state (arrival at a defined moment in time) without a durative process phase, similar to 'arrive', which is explicitly listed as an achievement verb in the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'the hour has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the arrival of a specific moment in time), representing a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual, bounded perceptual event marking the arrival/onset of a specific moment (the hour), which is a single completed occurrence without durative unfolding, thus failing the activity criterion.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'the hour has come' entails a transition into a new resulting state (the hour arriving/beginning), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has come' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness or completion of a change-of-state (the arrival of 'the hour'), which involves dynamic change and an inherent endpoint, incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the hour has come' presents a situation as actually occurring in the narrative world without any indication of counterfactuality; it is a factual statement within the reported speech.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come' expresses a positive eventuality with no negator directly negating it; the sentence contains no negation affecting this gram.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has come' entails a change of state (the hour transitioning from not-arrived to arrived), and that resulting state holds at the reference time, making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has come' encodes a change of state (the hour transitioning from not-yet-arrived to arrived) with the resulting state holding at the reference time, as indicated by the present-tense context and the imperative directives that follow.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has come' describes a punctual transition into a new state (the arrival of the hour) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative example 'I have broken the vase.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has come' denotes a transition event (arrival of the hour) construed as a bounded, completed whole without internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'the hour has come' asserts a realized fact in the actual world within the narrative context, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish operators applying to it.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has come' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'come' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has come' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'the hour' as the semantic agent/doer; it lacks the passive morphology of 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: We have heard his words against God . What is your opinion ? They all said he should be put to death .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' introduces an overt internal argument ('his words against God'), which is the patient/theme affected by the hearing event directed from the external argument (We).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the subject 'we' as agent/experiencer) and takes a direct object ('his words'), violating both the external argument and overt object conditions for ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument ('his words against God'), which is a direct object, violating the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'heard' denotes a punctual perceptual event—a near-instantaneous transition into awareness of the words—without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness of the words, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news' and satisfying the achievement criterion of instantaneous crossing into a new result state.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness of 'his words against God', which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a resulting state of knowledge/perception (having heard and retained the information) and unfolds as a durative experience rather than a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness (hearing his words), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, thus instantiating dynamic change incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'We have heard his words against God' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world, with no indication of counterfactuality or contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have heard' is not negated; the sentence 'We have heard his words against God' is a positive assertion with no negator present.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have heard' presents the event of hearing his words as an entry in the subject's life history without asserting that a resulting state (such as being in a state of having-heard) currently holds; it answers whether the event occurred, matching the experiential perfect.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' reports a past perceptual event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; hearing words does not create an enduring change of state comparable to breaking, receiving, or arriving.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (hearing his words) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the completed action of perceiving the utterance, not on a state or activity persisting from past to present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have heard' presents the auditory event as a bounded, completed transition (the moment of perceiving/receiving the words) rather than profiling internal temporal development, fitting the perfective construal.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'We have heard his words' asserts a realized, completed action in the actual world (present perfect indicating a past event with present relevance), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'heard', with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have heard' is active voice where the grammatical subject 'We' is the semantic agent (doer) and 'his words' is the grammatical object (semantic patient); passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle, which is absent here.

FullText: Pilate said : » Why , what evil has he done ? « But they cried out exceedingly , » Impale him ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has done' introduces an overt internal argument 'evil' (direct object), instantiating a two-participant event with an external argument (he) and an affected internal argument (evil).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he' as agent) and takes a direct object ('evil'), violating both conditions for ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has done' licenses an internal argument (the direct object 'evil'), violating the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has done (evil)' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous completed action without an inherent durative process phase; it refers to a discrete deed or act rather than an event that unfolds over measurable duration toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'has done evil' denotes a punctual transition into a state where evil has been committed, representing a completed change-of-state event at a definable threshold, regardless of any preparatory or durative phases of the underlying wrongdoing.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'has done evil' entails a specific, completed action with a defined result state (evil committed), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The perfective 'has done' entails a resulting state of evil having been committed, which constitutes a transition into a new state rather than a punctual event without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (the completion of an evil action) involving change-of-state and eventive progression, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'has done evil' as an actual question about what occurred in reality, not as a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negator 'what' does not negate the eventuality of 'has done'; rather, Pilate's question presupposes that he has done something evil and asks what that evil act is.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has done' presents the event as an entry in the subject's life history (what evil acts he has committed), without asserting that a resulting state currently holds; it answers whether the event has occurred, not what state persists.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has done' reports a past action without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it is a bare inquiry into what evil act occurred, not a statement that evil currently holds as a consequence.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (a specific evil action) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the action itself rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has done' presents the evil action as a bounded, completed transition without profiling its internal temporal development, fitting the perfective aspect definition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'what evil has he done' is a question seeking confirmation of an actual fact (whether evil was actually committed), asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world, not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'done', with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'he' corresponds to the semantic agent (doer) of 'done', and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' auxiliary); this is active voice.

FullText: At the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice , saying : » Eloi , Eloi , lama sabachthani ? « Interpreted it means , » My God , my God , why have you forsaken me ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' introduces an overt internal argument ('me'), encoding a two-participant event directed from the external argument (God) toward and affecting the internal argument (the speaker).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you') and introduces an overt direct object ('me'), violating both conditions for ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' licenses an internal argument ('me'), making it transitive and thus not a one-participant external-argument-only event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' denotes a punctual change-of-state (transition from not-abandoned to abandoned) without a durative process phase, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (abandonment/desertion) rather than an unfolding process, meeting the achievement criterion of a definable threshold crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (the state of being abandoned/deserted), which entails an inherent endpoint rather than a dynamic durative situation without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have forsaken' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not being forsaken to being forsaken) and does not represent a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the AktSemel criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a change-of-state event (abandonment/separation) involving dynamic progression from a prior state of presence to absence, which violates the stative requirement of no internal eventive unfolding.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'why have you forsaken me' is a factual utterance expressing Jesus's actual experience in the narrative moment, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have forsaken' is not negated; the negation marker 'why' is an interrogative adverb, not a negator that negates the eventuality of forsaking.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' entails a change of state (abandonment/withdrawal of divine presence) whose resulting state is relevant to the discourse context and Jesus's current suffering at the reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' encodes a change of state (from not-forsaken to forsaken) with the resulting state of abandonment persisting at the reference time of the utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' expresses a transition into a new state (abandonment) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the focus is on that change of state rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have forsaken' presents the transition of abandonment as a bounded, completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect intuition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'why have you forsaken me' is a direct utterance asserting a realized state of affairs (abandonment) in the actual world, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have forsaken' is a canonical present perfect formed with present-tense 'have' + past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, meeting the set_to_0_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have you forsaken' is active voice where the grammatical subject 'you' is the semantic agent (doer) and 'me' is the semantic patient; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence).

FullText: He bought a linen cloth , and taking him down , wound him in the linen cloth . Then he laid him in a tomb that had been cut out of a rock . He rolled a stone against the door of the tomb .
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'had been cut out of a rock' introduces an overt internal argument (the tomb) that is affected by the cutting action, satisfying the ASTrns feature requirement.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been cut'), and the lexical verb 'cut' licenses an external argument (an agent who performed the cutting), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'cut' licenses an internal argument (the tomb as theme/patient undergoing the change-of-state of being carved out), and the sole argument functions as an internal argument rather than an external agent.

FullText: They went to the tomb when the sun had risen . It was very early .

FullText: He said to them : » Do not be amazed . Jesus the Nazarene , who has been impaled , is not here . He has risen . Behold , the place where they laid him !

DtRes_explanation: The clause 'who has been impaled' is a stative copular construction describing a condition rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state that persists at reference time; the impalement is a past event being characterized, not a current state in effect.

FullText: She told those who had been with him , for they mourned and wept .

TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had risen' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes past tense specification distinct from the simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.

DtExp_explanation: The resulting state of Jesus having spoken is relevant for the subsequent narrative events (being received into heaven and sitting at God's right hand), making this a narrative progression event rather than a life-history experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'had spoken to them' reports a past interaction without entailing a persisting resulting state at the reference time; the focus is on the temporal sequence of events (speaking occurred before Jesus was received into heaven) rather than on a state that holds at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (speaking to them) that serves as a temporal boundary for subsequent events, rather than a situation holding continuously across time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had spoken' presents the speech act as a bounded, completed transition event without profiling its internal temporal development, meeting the core criterion for TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'had spoken' is a past perfect form asserting a realized event in the actual world (Jesus speaking to them before his ascension), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.

FullText: Many have attempted to write an orderly narrative about the things that have taken place .

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'attempt' denotes an activity or effort without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; the clause reports a past action without indicating that a change of state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have attempted' describes a habitual or iterative series of attempts extending from a past point to the reference time, expressing a continuous situation rather than discrete bounded events.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' presents the transition of attempting as a bounded, completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect definition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (many people have actually attempted this), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have attempted' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'attempted', with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.

FullText: Many have attempted to write an orderly narrative about the things that have taken place .

ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (things that have taken place), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.

DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'have taken place' presents events as part of a life history without asserting that a resulting state currently holds; it answers whether events have occurred, focusing on their occurrence rather than any persistent consequence.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have taken place' describes past events without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it is an activity/occurrence verb similar to 'visit' or 'happen' that does not encode a transition to a new state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have taken place' describes discrete bounded events (things that occurred) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the verb 'take place' entails punctual occurrences rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have taken place' presents the transition of events occurring as a bounded whole without profiling their internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have taken place' asserts realized events in the actual world as a factual statement, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish contexts applied.

FullText: Most excellent Theophilus Greek : Theo philos : friend of God : I have accurately traced the course of all things from the beginning . Now it seems good to me to write this report to you .

AktAch_explanation: The event 'have accurately traced the course of all things from the beginning' denotes a punctual transition into a completed state of knowledge/understanding, representing the boundary point of achieving comprehensive understanding rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'traced' with the internal argument 'the course of all things from the beginning' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (completing the tracing of a bounded, defined course), and the situation can be modified by 'in X time' (e.g., 'traced in many years'), indicating a bounded event rather than a dynamic activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have traced' entails a resulting state (completion of tracing the course of all things) and unfolds over a measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing both criteria for semelfactive classification.

ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world ('I have accurately traced'), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have (accurately) traced' is presented as a realized, positive event with no negator directly negating it; the sentence structure is affirmative.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have traced' presents the completed action of tracing as part of the speaker's life history without asserting that a resulting state (being traced) currently holds; the focus is on the occurrence of the investigative act itself, supported by the adverbial 'accurately' which modifies the manner of the completed action rather than indicating duration.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have traced' encodes a completed transition from a state of not having traced to having traced the course of all things, with the resulting state (possession of accurate knowledge/completion of the investigation) persisting at the reference time, supporting the subsequent action of writing the report.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have traced' describes a completed investigative action with focus on the accomplishment of tracing the course, not a continuous state or activity holding over time; this is a bounded achievement rather than a situation holding continuously from past to present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have traced' presents the action of tracing as a completed, bounded whole event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect definition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have accurately traced' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (present perfect indicating completed action), not projected as hypothetical, conditional, or desired.

VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (doer) of the action 'traced', and 'the course of all things' is the grammatical object (semantic patient); this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no 'been' auxiliary and no passive morphology.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has heard' asserts God's action of hearing the prayer as a realized fact in the actual world within the angel's speech, not as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has heard' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'heard' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
