FullText: » He will go before God in the spirit and power of Elijah . He will turn the hearts of fathers to their children and wrongdoers to the way of righteousness . This will make ready a people whose hearts have been directed to Jehovah . «

DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have been directed' describes a state (hearts oriented toward Jehovah) that holds continuously from a past point to the reference time, rather than a discrete bounded event or transition.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive construction 'have been directed' presents a bounded transition (the directing of hearts to Jehovah) as a completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have been directed to Jehovah' asserts a realized state of affairs as the result of the preparatory action ('This will make ready a people'), describing an actual condition of the people being prepared, not a projected or hypothetical situation.

FullText: The angel answered : » I am Gabriel , whose place is before God . I have been sent to give you this good news .

FullText: » You will be without voice or language till the day when these things happen . Your lack of faith in what I say has caused this . «

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'caused' licenses an external argument (your lack of faith) as the agent/initiator and introduces an internal argument (this), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the one-participant internal-argument-only requirement.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'caused' licenses an internal argument ('this'), violating the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'cause' denotes a punctual change-of-state or transition (bringing about a result) without a durative process phase; the causation itself is instantaneous rather than unfolding over measurable time.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has caused this' denotes a punctual transition into a result state (the loss of voice/language), representing the boundary point where the lack of faith brought about a new condition, rather than an unfolding process.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has caused this' entails a resulting change of state (loss of voice/language), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has caused this' denotes a dynamic change-of-state event (causation of a condition) involving internal eventive unfolding and an inherent endpoint, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'caused' entails a change of state (the subject's loss of voice/language), and that resulting state holds at the reference time, making this non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has caused' encodes a transition from a prior state (faith present) to a resulting state (lack of voice/language) that persists at the reference time, as evidenced by the consequential condition described in the preceding clause.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has caused' describes a discrete bounded event (the causation of a specific consequence) with focus on the transition/result rather than a continuous situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has caused this' presents the causation as a bounded, completed transition event (lack of faith → resulting condition), construing it as a single whole rather than profiling internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has caused this' asserts a realized fact in the actual world—the speaker is explaining the actual cause of the current situation, not projecting a hypothetical or non-actual scenario.

VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'Your lack of faith in what I say' is the semantic agent (causer) of the event, and 'this' is the grammatical object functioning as the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: She said : » This is the way God has dealt with me by taking away my reproach among men . «

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has dealt with' entails a change of state (removal of reproach) that results in a current state of affairs (reproach is gone), which persists at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'happened' is an activity/event verb that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; the shepherds want to 'see this thing that has happened,' treating it as a past occurrence to be witnessed, not as a state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'that has happened' describes a discrete bounded event (the birth/arrival of Jesus) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for punctual events.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has happened' presents the transition event of something occurring as a bounded, completed whole without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect criterion.

FullText: As soon as they saw it they told what they had heard about the child .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'they') as the experiencer/agent of the hearing event, and the clause contains an overt direct object 'what', violating both conditions for ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument ('what they had heard about the child'), violating the requirement for zero internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'hear' denotes a punctual, instantaneous transition into awareness without a durative process phase; the event is construed as a single moment of perception rather than an unfolding process culminating in a defined result state.
AktAch_explanation: The past perfect 'had heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness of information about the child, representing an instantaneous crossing into a new cognitive state regardless of any preceding listening process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (hearing the information about the child), which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had heard' entails a resulting state of knowledge/information that persists, and hearing unfolds over a measurable duration rather than as a single punctual occurrence, making it a durative activity rather than a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness (hearing news about the child), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what they had heard about the child' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the hearing occurred before the telling), not to indicate a counterfactual situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had heard' is not negated; the negation rule set_to_0_if does not apply, and the eventuality of hearing is presented as realized, not absent.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had heard' presents the event of hearing as part of the subject's prior life history without asserting that a resulting state currently holds; it answers whether the event occurred, not whether a state persists at reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had heard' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it merely reports a past interaction of acquiring information, similar to the negative example 'He has given me advice before.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (hearing about the child at some prior point) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the completed action of hearing, not on a state persisting from past to reference time.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'what they had heard about the child' is embedded within a reported speech context ('they told what...') that asserts a realized fact in the actual world; the past perfect 'had heard' describes a completed event prior to the telling, not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had heard' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes a tense specification other than the simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.

AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'heard' denotes a punctual perceptual event—a near-instantaneous transition into awareness—without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, thus instantiating achievement rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (the moment of perceiving/learning the things), which is a single boundary transition into a new cognitive state, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news.'
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness, which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for perceptual events.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had heard' entails a resulting state of knowledge/perception and unfolds over a measurable duration (the shepherds heard things during their experience), not a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness, which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'had heard' as a factual past event that actually occurred (the shepherds genuinely heard things), with no indication of counterfactuality; it uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative without contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had heard' is not negated; the sentence is positive and describes what the shepherds actually heard and saw.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had heard' presents an event as part of the shepherds' life history (what they experienced) without asserting that a resulting state of hearing currently holds; it answers whether the event occurred, supported by the frequency context 'all the things.'
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had heard' reports a past perception event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it merely describes what the shepherds experienced in the past, similar to the negative example 'They have visited Paris.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (things heard and seen) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the specific occurrences that prompted the shepherds' praise.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had heard' presents the hearing as a bounded, completed event prior to the shepherds' return, construing the transition of coming to know something as a single whole without internal temporal development.

TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had heard' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes tense specification other than simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'they' is the semantic agent (doer) of the hearing, and 'had heard' lacks passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence); this is active voice, not passive.

AktState_explanation: The gram 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness, which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion.

DtRes_explanation: The clause 'they had heard and seen' reports past perceptual events without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it merely describes what the shepherds experienced, similar to the negative example 'They have visited Paris.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (specific things heard and seen) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the completion of these events rather than their duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had seen' presents the act of seeing as a bounded, completed transition event prior to the shepherds' return, without profiling internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The past perfect 'had seen' asserts a realized fact in the actual world—the shepherds actually witnessed and experienced these things—and is not embedded in a modal, conditional, future, or wish context.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (instantaneous moment of visual perception) rather than a durative process, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news.'

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have seen' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it reports a past perceptual experience without a change of state currently in effect.

TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have seen' presents the visual perception as a bounded, completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The present perfect 'have seen' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (a completed action with present relevance), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.

FullText: She had been a widow eighty-four years . She never left the temple . She worshipped and fasted with supplications day and night .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'be' functions as a copula linking the subject 'She' to the predicative complement 'a widow', rather than introducing an internal argument affected by the subject.
ASUncc_explanation: The copular verb 'be' in 'had been a widow' encodes a one-participant stative event where the sole argument (She) functions as an internal argument (theme), with no external argument licensed, matching the positive example of copular 'be' clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'be' with predicate adjective 'a widow' denotes a state of existence/identity where the sole argument 'She' functions as an internal argument (theme) rather than an external argument (agent), matching the set_to_0_if criterion for existence and appearance verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The past perfect progressive 'had been a widow' denotes a durative state extending over eighty-four years with an inherent endpoint (the present moment of the narrative), satisfying the requirement for a process phase leading to a defined terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The event unfolds over a measurable duration (eighty-four years) rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and the past perfect progressive construction emphasizes the durative state rather than a punctual transition.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had been a widow' denotes a state resulting from a bounded event (becoming a widow) and lacks dynamic unfolding or sustained engagement, thus failing the activity criterion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been a widow' entails a resulting state (widowhood) and unfolds over a measurable duration (eighty-four years), failing both criteria for semelfactive classification.
AktState_explanation: The gram is morphologically marked as progressive aspect ('had been'), which entails dynamic unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual state in the actual world (she was a widow for eighty-four years) using past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within narrative, without any indication of counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been a widow' is a positive assertion of a state that occurred and held for eighty-four years; no negator negates this eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb entails a change of status (becoming a widow) that remains in effect at the reference time, and the duration adverbial 'eighty-four years' indicates a continuing situation rather than a completed life experience.
DtRes_explanation: The stative copula 'had been a widow' describes a persisting state rather than encoding a transition; it does not entail a resulting state that holds at the reference time but rather a continuous condition, and the duration adverbial 'eighty-four years' indicates a continuing situation rather than a completed transition.
DtUniv_explanation: The gram 'had been a widow' expresses a state that held continuously for eighty-four years from a past starting point, matching the intuition of a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'be a widow' is a stative situation without a construed boundary; the clause with duration adverbial 'eighty-four years' makes the internal temporal span accessible rather than packaging it as a bounded whole, and the pluperfect progressive construal profiles the ongoing state rather than a transition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (she was a widow for eighty-four years), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish contexts present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been a widow' is a past perfect form (had + past participle 'been'), which encodes past tense specification other than simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the noun phrase 'a widow' (non-verbal predicate), making this a copular construction rather than a passive voice construction; passive voice requires 'been' + past participle functioning as a verbal predicate.

FullText: When they had accomplished all things that were according to the law of God they returned to Galilee , to their own city Nazareth .

AktAch_explanation: The event 'had accomplished all things' denotes a punctual transition into a completed result state (all required tasks finished), marking a definable threshold after which they returned to Galilee, fitting the achievement criterion of a boundary-point transition rather than an unfolding process.

AktState_explanation: The gram 'had accomplished' denotes a bounded dynamic event with an inherent endpoint (completion of actions), involving internal eventive unfolding and change-of-state, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.

DtRes_explanation: The clause 'had accomplished all things' is a temporal subordinate clause establishing a prior completed action that triggers the main event ('they returned'); the resulting state of accomplishment does not persist at the reference time because the narrative moves forward to a new event, and the construction functions as a temporal sequencer rather than encoding a resultative state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (accomplishing all things according to the law) with a clear completion point, after which they returned, rather than a situation holding continuously over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The pluperfect 'had accomplished' presents the completion of all required actions as a bounded whole event that serves as the temporal boundary for the subsequent return to Galilee, instantiating perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'had accomplished all things' is embedded in a temporal when-clause that asserts a realized sequence of events in the actual world (they accomplished things, then they returned), not a hypothetical or projected situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had accomplished' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes tense specification other than simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.

FullText: When they saw him they were astonished . His mother said to him : » Son , why did you treat us this way ? Your father and I have searched for you and we have been worried . «

DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have searched' expresses a continuous activity (searching) that has held from a past starting point up to the reference time, denoting duration rather than discrete bounded events.

FullText: When they saw him they were astonished . His mother said to him : » Son , why did you treat us this way ? Your father and I have searched for you and we have been worried . «

FullText: John reproved Herod the tetrarch for all the evil things he had done for he took his brother Herodias’ wife .

AktAch_explanation: The event 'had done (evil things)' unfolds over a measurable duration as a series of actions rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and it can naturally be conceived as the completion of durative processes (e.g., 'he finished doing evil things').

AktState_explanation: The gram 'had done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (past perfect marking completion of an action), involving internal eventive unfolding and change, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.

TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' presents the evil things as a bounded, completed action without internal temporal development, construing the situation as a discrete whole event prior to the reproval.

VcPass_explanation: The target 'had done' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' functioning as the semantic agent (doer); there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence) and the semantic patient 'evil things' appears as the grammatical object, not subject.

FullText: Then the devil said : » I will give all this authority and glory to you , for it has been turned over to me . I may give it to whomever I will .
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'has been turned over to me' contains an overt internal argument ('it', the direct object of the active voice), and the passive voice preserves the two-participant transitive structure with an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been turned over'), and the lexical verb 'turn over' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent performing the turning over), which violates the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'turned over' licenses an internal argument (the implicit object 'it'/'authority and glory'), and the sole explicit argument functions as a theme/patient undergoing change rather than as an external agent.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'turned over' denotes a punctual change-of-state (transfer of authority from one party to another) that occurs at a boundary moment without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for instantaneous boundary transitions.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has been turned over' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (authority passing from one possessor to another), representing a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.

AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been turned over' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('has been'), which entails dynamic unfolding and change-of-state (authority transferred from one party to another), making it incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (the authority and glory have been turned over to the devil), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been turned over' is not negated; the negator does not apply to this eventuality, which is presented as a realized state in the devil's assertion.

DtRes_explanation: The clause 'it has been turned over to me' encodes a change of state (from not being in the devil's possession to being in the devil's possession) with the resulting state persisting at the reference time, entailing that the authority and glory currently remain under the devil's control.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has been turned over' describes a transition into a new state (authority being transferred to the devil) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative example 'I have eaten the rice (and now it is gone).'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has been turned over' presents a passive bounded transition (the act of transferring authority) as a completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has been turned over to me' asserts a realized fact in the actual world within the devil's statement of what is currently true, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been turned over' contains progressive marking ('been' + past participle 'turned'), which constitutes additional marking beyond the simple present perfect form 'has' + past participle, thus instantiating TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has been turned over' contains the passive morphology (auxiliary 'has been' + past participle 'turned'), the grammatical subject 'it' is the semantic patient (the thing turned over), and the agent is omitted, satisfying all criteria for VcPass.

FullText: When the devil had completed every temptation he departed from him for a season .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'completed' licenses an external argument (the devil as agent) and takes a direct object (every temptation), violating both conditions for ASUncc.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'completed' denotes the finishing of a durative process (the series of temptations), which naturally occurs as the complement of 'finish' and thus indicates a process phase rather than a punctual boundary transition.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails a specific, inherent endpoint (completion of every temptation) and a defined result state, making it a bounded accomplishment rather than an unbounded activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had completed every temptation' entails a transition into a resulting state (completion of all temptations), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had completed' denotes a bounded dynamic event with an inherent endpoint (the finishing of temptations), involving change-of-state and eventive progression, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence (the devil completed temptations before departing), without any indication that the situation is contrary to fact; it presents an event that actually occurred in the narrative world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had completed' expresses a realized event (the devil did complete every temptation); there is no negator negating this eventuality, so ClNegPol is not instantiated.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'completed' entails a change of state (temptation goes from ongoing to finished), and this resulting state is relevant for the subsequent narrative event ('he departed'), making it non-experiential per the edge case rule on narrative progression.
DtRes_explanation: The pluperfect 'had completed' encodes a transition from an incomplete to a completed state of temptation, with the resulting state (temptations finished) holding at the reference time of the main clause, enabling the subsequent departure.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (completion of temptations) with a clear transition point, after which the devil departed, rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had completed' presents the transition of finishing every temptation as a bounded, discrete event with a clear endpoint, meeting the core criterion of TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The past perfect 'had completed' asserts a realized event in the actual world within a narrative sequence; the temporal clause establishes a factual prerequisite for the main clause 'he departed,' both describing completed events rather than projecting hypothetical or non-actual situations.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had completed' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes tense specification other than simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'the devil' is the semantic agent (doer) of the action, and 'every temptation' is the grammatical object functioning as the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no auxiliary 'been' + past participle structure.

FullText: » The Spirit of Jehovah is upon me . He anointed me to preach good news to the poor . He has sent me to proclaim release to the captives , and recovering of sight to the blind . To set at liberty those who are bruised .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has sent' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'He') and introduces an overt direct object ('me'), violating both conditions in set_to_0_if.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (the speaker being in a sent/commissioned state), representing an instantaneous boundary event rather than an unfolding process.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has sent me' entails a resulting change of state (transition into a state of being sent/commissioned) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence but rather as a completed action with consequent result conditions.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has sent' denotes a bounded dynamic event (sending) with an inherent endpoint and involves change-of-state, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He has sent me' presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (a factual past/present perfect statement), with no indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has sent' expresses a positive eventuality with no negator directly negating it; the sentence is affirmative.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has sent' entails a change of state (the subject being in a sent/commissioned state) that results in an ongoing purpose or mission that holds at the reference time, making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has sent' encodes a change of state (from not-sent to sent) with a resulting state that persists at the reference time: the speaker is currently in the state of being sent on a mission to proclaim and set at liberty, which is a binding directive currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has sent' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of sending/commissioning) rather than a continuous state or activity holding over time, and the focus is on the transition into the state of being sent rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has sent' presents the sending as a bounded transition event (a completed action of dispatch) rather than profiling its internal temporal development, consistent with the perfective aspect intuition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'He has sent me' asserts a realized action in the actual world as part of a statement of fact about the speaker's mission, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.

VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'He' is the semantic agent (doer) of the verb 'sent', and 'me' is the grammatical object functioning as the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no auxiliary 'been' + past participle morphology.

FullText: » What have we to do with you Jesus , you Nazarene ? Have you come to destroy us ? I know who you are . You are the Holy One of God ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it only has an external argument (you) and an infinitival complement expressing purpose, not an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' encodes a one-participant change of location event where the sole argument 'you' functions as an internal argument (theme undergoing motion), with no external argument (agent/initiator) licensed by the verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument 'you' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing movement, not as an external argument (agent/initiator).

AktAch_explanation: The target 'have come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location/situation), representing an instantaneous boundary crossing regardless of any preparatory travel process, consistent with the positive examples 'arrived at noon' and 'reached the summit'.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of arrival at a location with an inherent endpoint, not a dynamic durative activity without defined result state.

AktState_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving change-of-state (arrival/movement to a location), which instantiates dynamic progression rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Have you come to destroy us?' is an open conditional interrogative asking about a present/actual situation, not a counterfactual; it presents a possible real-world scenario without indicating the event is contrary to fact.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have come' is a motion verb where the mover (you/Jesus) has arrived but the resulting state of presence does not persist at the reference time in the narrative context; this is an experiential/interrogative use asking about purpose rather than encoding a persisting resultant state.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have come' describes a discrete bounded event (arrival/transition into a new state) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, matching the negative example 'I have broken the vase.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have come' presents the transition of arrival as a bounded whole event without profiling internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have come' appears in an interrogative form ('Have you come to destroy us?') that projects a hypothetical purpose or outcome rather than asserting a realized fact, triggering irrealis.

FullText: Jesus rebuked him , saying : » Be quiet and come out of him . « When the demon had thrown him down in front of them , he came out of him without harming him .

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had thrown him down' entails a change of location (the subject is thrown to the ground), and this resulting state is relevant for the subsequent narrative event ('he came out of him'), making it non-experiential per the edge case rule about narrative progression.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had thrown him down' encodes a change of state (from upright to down position) with the resulting state persisting at the reference time of the narrative sequence, as evidenced by the subsequent clause 'he came out of him' which describes the demon's exit following this completed action.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the demon throwing him down) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the verb entails a transition into a new state.

FullText: He saw two boats docked by the shore . The fishermen had gone away from them and were washing their nets .

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had gone away' is a positive statement describing a realized event; no negator appears in the sentence to negate this eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had gone away' entails a change of location (fishermen leaving the boats), and the resulting state (being away from the boats) persists at the reference time, as evidenced by the subsequent clause 'were washing their nets' which describes their current activity at a distance from the boats.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'had gone away' describes a motion verb where the mover (fishermen) has returned to a different state (washing nets at the reference time), indicating no persisting resulting state from the departure itself.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the fishermen departing from the boats) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the transition into a new state of being away.

FullText: Everyone was amazed and they glorified God . They were filled with fear . They said : » We have seen strange things today . «

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an internal argument ('strange things'), violating the requirement for ASUnrg which demands no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a punctual perceptual event—a near-instantaneous transition into visual awareness—without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, matching the achievement category.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness of strange things, representing a single boundary moment of discovery rather than an unfolding durative process.

DtExp_explanation: The clause 'We have seen strange things today' presents the event of witnessing as an entry in the subject's life history (what occurred during this day) without asserting that a resulting state of 'having seen' currently holds beyond the experiential fact itself, matching the positive example 'We have seen the Eiffel Tower.'
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have seen' reports a past perceptual event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it is an experiential perfect indicating that the event occurred, not that a change of state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events ('strange things') that occurred during a specific time period ('today') rather than a continuous situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have seen' presents the perceptual event as a bounded, completed whole without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect construal of a transition/accomplishment event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'We have seen strange things today' is asserted as a realized fact within the actual world of the narrative—the speakers are reporting what they have actually witnessed, not projecting a hypothetical or non-actual situation.

TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have received' presents the transition of receiving a reward as a bounded, completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'You have received your reward' asserts a realized fact in the actual world as a completed action, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish contexts applied.

FullText: They were all afraid and they praised God . They said : » A great prophet has appeared among us . God has visited inspected selected his people . «

TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'seven demons had gone out' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (a past event that occurred), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had gone out' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes past tense marking beyond the simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'had gone out' lacks the required passive morphology (have + been + past participle); it is an active perfect construction where the grammatical subject 'seven demons' is the semantic agent performing the action of going out.

FullText: » Those by the side of the road are they who have heard . Then the devil comes and takes the word from their hearts that they may not believe and be saved .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' lacks an overt internal argument (no direct object specifying what was heard), instantiating an activity reading where the affected patient is not syntactically realized.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have heard' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'they' who perform the action of hearing), making it a two-participant event rather than a one-participant internal-argument-only event.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument (the word, understood from context as the direct object), violating the requirement for zero internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual, instantaneous transition into the state of awareness/perception without a durative process phase, classifying it as an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (matching the positive example 'They have heard the news'), representing an instantaneous crossing of the threshold from not-knowing to knowing, rather than an unfolding durative process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness, which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation, matching the negative example 'They have heard the blasphemy.'
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having heard to having heard knowledge/information), which violates the AktSemel requirement of no resulting state change.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (hearing/receiving the word), which involves eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who have heard' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (people have heard the word), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have heard' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('may not believe') applies to a different eventuality ('believe'), not to the hearing event.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have heard' presents the event of hearing as an occurrence in the subject's life history without asserting that a resulting state (such as continued possession of the word) holds at reference time; the subsequent clause about the devil taking the word away confirms the focus is on the past event of hearing rather than a current state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' reports a past perceptual event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; hearing is a bare perception event that does not encode a transition to a new state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the act of hearing) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the completion of the hearing event itself, not on a state persisting from past to present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have heard' presents the act of hearing as a bounded, completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect intuition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'who have heard' asserts a realized state in the actual world as a defining characteristic of those people, not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'heard' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have heard' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'they' as the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of hearing; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle, which is absent here.

FullText: When he arrived on the shore , a demon-possessed man met him . He wore no clothes and did not live in a house but in the tombs . He had been this way a long time .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had been' functions as a copula linking the subject to the predicative complement 'this way', introducing no overt internal argument (patient/theme) and thus does not instantiate ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'had been' with the predicate adjective 'this way' licenses an external argument (the man as agent/experiencer in a state), and the clause structure involves a copular construction with an external argument rather than a one-participant internal-argument-only event.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had been' denotes existence or a state of being without projecting an external argument; the sole argument (he) functions as an internal argument (theme) rather than an external argument (agent/initiator).
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'had been this way' denotes a stative condition (existing in a particular state/manner) without an inherent durative process leading to a defined terminal point; it describes a prolonged state rather than an accomplishment with culmination.
AktAch_explanation: The event unfolds over a measurable duration ('a long time') rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and the perfect aspect indicates a state resulting from a durative process rather than a punctual transition.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had been this way' denotes a state of being in a particular condition without dynamic unfolding or sustained engagement, lacking the dynamic durative character required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been this way' unfolds over a measurable duration ('a long time') rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, making it a durative state rather than a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been this way' is morphologically marked as progressive aspect (past perfect progressive), which entails dynamic unfolding and internal eventive development over time, making it incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual situation in the actual world (the man had actually been in this state for a long time) without any indication of counterfactuality; it uses past perfect for temporal anteriority within a narrative.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been this way' expresses a positive state (the man had existed in this condition), and no negator directly negates this eventuality; the negations in the sentence ('wore no clothes', 'did not live') apply to different predicates.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'He had been this way a long time' contains a duration adverbial ('a long time') indicating a continuing situation rather than a completed life experience, and the resulting state of being in that condition persists at the reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The stative copula 'had been [this way]' describes a persisting condition rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; it reports a state that held over a duration ('a long time') without entailing a change of state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'had been this way a long time' expresses a state (being in a particular condition/manner) that held continuously from a past starting point up to the reference time, with explicit duration marking ('a long time'), matching the pattern of continuous situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'He had been this way a long time' denotes a stative situation (a persistent state or condition) without a construed boundary, and the duration adverbial 'a long time' makes the internal temporal span accessible rather than packaging the situation as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'He had been this way a long time' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world as part of the narrative description, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applied.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been' is a past perfect form (past-tense 'had' + past participle 'been'), which encodes tense specification other than simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adverbial phrase 'this way' (not a verbal past participle), making 'been' a lexical copula rather than a passive auxiliary, so VcPass is not instantiated.

FullText: Jesus asked him : » What is your name ? « He answered : » Legion « for many demons had entered into him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' introduces an overt internal argument 'him' (indirect object via the preposition 'into'), encoding a two-participant event where demons (external argument) are directed toward and affect him (internal argument).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'entered into' licenses an external argument (the demons as agent/initiator), which violates the requirement for a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'entered into' licenses an internal argument ('him'), and the sole argument (demons) functions as the external argument of a change-of-location event where the theme moves into a location, violating the one-participant external-argument-only requirement.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'enter into' denotes a punctual boundary transition (crossing a threshold into the body) without a durative process phase, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for instantaneous change-of-state events.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had entered into him' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (demons now inhabiting the person), representing a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had entered into him' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the demons completing their entry into him) and a defined result state (demons now being inside him), making it a bounded event rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had entered into him' entails a transition into a new resulting state (demons being inside him), which violates the core requirement that semelfactive events must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had entered into' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (demons moving into a person) with a clear endpoint and internal eventive progression, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual situation that actually occurred in the narrative world (many demons had entered into him), using past perfect for temporal anteriority within the narrative, not to express a counterfactual condition.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had entered into (him)' is not negated; the negator does not apply to this eventuality—the sentence affirms that demons had entered into him.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'enter into' entails a change of location/state (demons entering the subject), and this resulting state (demons being inside him) holds at the reference time and is relevant for the narrative context explaining his condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' encodes a change of state (demons transitioning from outside to inside him) with the resulting state persisting at the reference time of the narrative, indicating a completed transition with current effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' describes a discrete bounded event (demons entering him) rather than a continuous state or activity holding over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for punctual transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had entered into' presents the transition of demons entering him as a bounded, completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, fitting the perfective aspect definition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'many demons had entered into him' asserts a realized fact in the actual world as the explanation for why the man's name is 'Legion'; it is not projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had entered into' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes past tense specification distinct from the simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'had entered into him' is active voice where the grammatical subject 'many demons' is the semantic agent (doer), lacking the passive morphology of 'have been' + past participle required for VcPass.

FullText: They went out to see what happened . They came to Jesus and found the man who had been demon possessed . He was clothed , in his right mind and sitting at the feet of Jesus . They were afraid .
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'had been demon possessed' introduces an overt internal argument (the man/subject), encoding a two-participant event where the man is affected by possession, satisfying the ASTrns feature.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been possessed'), and the lexical verb 'possess' licenses an external argument (the demon as agent, whether suppressed or implied), violating the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses with external arguments.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'possessed' denotes a state or condition (being possessed by a demon) in which the sole argument 'the man' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing or experiencing the state, not as an external argument (agent/initiator).
AktAcc_explanation: The past perfect 'had been demon possessed' describes a state or condition rather than a dynamic event with a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; possession is a static resultant state, not an accomplishment with inherent temporal unfolding toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The past perfect 'had been demon possessed' describes a state or condition that unfolded over a measurable duration rather than a punctual transition, and does not denote a single boundary crossing into a new result state.
AktAct_explanation: The situation denotes a bounded result state (being possessed) rather than a dynamic durative activity; possession is a stative condition without sustained dynamic engagement, and the past perfect aspect marks completion of the state prior to the narrative moment.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been demon possessed' describes a resulting state condition (the man's condition of being possessed) rather than a punctual event without consequent result, and the past perfect construes it as a durative state preceding the discovery, not a single bounded instantaneous occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been demon possessed' is morphologically marked with progressive aspect ('had been'), which entails dynamic unfolding and internal eventive change incompatible with stative readings per CRI set_to_0_if rule 2.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the man had been possessed before they found him), describing a factual situation that actually occurred, with no indication of counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been demon possessed' is not negated; it describes a positive state that the man actually experienced, as evidenced by the following clause describing his current condition after this state ended.
DtExp_explanation: The verb entails a change of state (possession) whose resulting condition is relevant to the narrative context—the man's current state of being no longer possessed is central to the subsequent description of his clothed and mentally sound condition.
DtRes_explanation: The stative copula 'had been' describes a persisting condition rather than encoding a transition; it reports the man's state at a past reference time without entailing that the possessed state holds at the current reference time (the narrative present shows he is now clothed and in his right mind, indicating the possession has ended).
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded state (demon possession) that has ended, with focus on the transition from possessed to no longer possessed (now clothed and in his right mind), rather than a situation holding continuously across time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'had been demon possessed' presents a bounded transition (entering into a state of possession) as a completed whole event, with the internal development of the possession not made accessible; the clause describes a discrete state change that occurred prior to the narrative moment.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been demon possessed' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world—the man's past condition of possession is presented as a fact that occurred, not as hypothetical, conditional, or otherwise non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been' is a past perfect progressive form (past tense 'had' + progressive 'been'), which encodes tense specification other than simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adjectival predicate 'demon possessed' (a non-verbal AP), making 'been' a copular verb rather than a passive auxiliary, so VcPass is not instantiated.

FullText: » Return to your house and declare the great things God has done for you . « He went throughout the whole city telling the great things Jesus had done for him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has done' introduces an overt internal argument 'the great things' (direct object), instantiating a two-participant event directed from the external argument (God) toward and affecting the internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (God as agent) and introduces an overt indirect object ('for you'), violating both conditions in set_to_0_if.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('the great things'), making it transitive and thus not a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'done' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous completion of an action without a durative process phase; it marks a boundary transition into a completed state rather than an event that unfolds over measurable duration with an inherent endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has done (for you)' refers to a range of unspecified actions or miracles that unfold over time rather than a single punctual transition, as evidenced by the plural 'great things' and the context of declaring multiple accomplishments.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails specific, inherent endpoints or defined result states (the great things God accomplished), and the verb denotes completed bounded events rather than a dynamic durative activity without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has done' entails resulting changes of state (miracles, healings, or transformative acts) and does not construe as a single punctual occurrence without consequent result conditions.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a bounded dynamic event with internal eventive unfolding and a clear endpoint, involving active progression from non-completion to completion, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (God has done great things), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has done' expresses a positive eventuality with no negator directly negating it; the sentence structure is affirmative.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has done' entails a change of state (great things accomplished for the subject) whose resulting effects remain relevant at the reference time, as evidenced by the imperative to 'declare' these things, indicating the results persist and are meant to be communicated.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has done' reports past actions/miracles without entailing a persisting resulting state at the reference time; it is a narrative report of completed events rather than a transition to a state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (the great things God has done) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, making it a punctual accomplishment rather than a durative state.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The perfective 'has done' presents the action of God performing great things as a bounded, completed whole event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the intuition for TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target gram 'has done' asserts God's actions as realized facts in the actual world within a narrative context, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish operators present.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'done' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'God' corresponds to the semantic agent (doer) of 'has done', and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); this is active voice.

FullText: » Return to your house and declare the great things God has done for you . « He went throughout the whole city telling the great things Jesus had done for him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had done' introduces an overt internal argument ('the great things'), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant event with an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'had done' licenses an external argument (Jesus as agent) and introduces an overt direct object ('the great things'), violating both conditions in set_to_0_if.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('the great things'), violating the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a durative process of performing miracles or acts of healing that necessarily culminated in a defined result state (the great things accomplished), fitting the accomplishment profile.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had done for him' refers to an extended series of miraculous actions and healings (contextually implied by 'great things'), which unfolds over a measurable duration rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, thus failing the punctual transition criterion.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had done for him' entails a specific, inherent endpoint or defined result state (the completion of miraculous acts), and the verb denotes a bounded event that can be modified by 'in X time' adverbials, failing the criteria for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had done' entails a resulting change of state (Jesus performed miraculous deeds that transformed the man's condition), and the context construes it as a completed action with consequent results rather than a single punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (miracles/actions performed by Jesus) involving internal eventive unfolding and change, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual past event (Jesus's miracles) that actually occurred in the narrative world, with no indication of counterfactuality; it uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within the narrative without contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had done' expresses a realized event (Jesus performed great things); no negator negates this eventuality, so ClNegPol is not instantiated.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'Jesus had done for him' describes miraculous deeds that constitute a resulting state of changed circumstances (healing/restoration) relevant to the narrative context of the man declaring these things throughout the city, making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'Jesus had done for him' reports a past interaction (miraculous healing) without entailing a persisting resulting state at the reference time; it is used to describe what the man is telling others about, not a state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (the great things Jesus had done) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on specific accomplishments rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' presents the action of Jesus performing great things as a bounded, completed whole event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target gram asserts a realized fact in the actual world—Jesus's past actions are presented as completed events that occurred, not as hypothetical, conditional, or otherwise non-actual situations.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had done' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes past tense specification beyond the simple present perfect, matching the positive example 'I had known him.'
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'Jesus' is the semantic agent (doer) of the action 'had done', and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence); this is active voice.

FullText: He said to her : » Daughter , your faith has made you whole ; go in peace . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has made' introduces an overt internal argument 'you' (direct object), encoding a two-participant event where the external argument (your faith) affects the internal argument (you), satisfying the ASTrns feature.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an external argument (your faith as agent/initiator) and introduces an overt direct object (you), violating both conditions in set_to_0_if.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an internal argument ('you') and denotes a change-of-state event where the sole argument functions as a theme/patient rather than an external agent.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has made (you) whole' denotes a punctual change-of-state or boundary transition (from not-whole to whole) without a durative process phase, fitting the set_to_0_if criterion for instantaneous change-of-state events.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has made you whole' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (from not-whole to whole), representing a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails a specific, inherent endpoint and defined result state ('whole'), and the verb denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of a state change rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has made you whole' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from being unwell/broken to being whole), which violates the core requirement that semelfactive events must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has made' denotes a change-of-state event (transitioning from not whole to whole) involving dynamic progression and an inherent endpoint, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (the daughter's faith has made her whole), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has made (you) whole' is a positive assertion with no negator present in the sentence; the eventuality is construed as realized, not absent from reality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has made you whole' entails a change of state (from not whole to whole) where the resulting state of wholeness holds at the reference time, satisfying the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has made you whole' encodes a transition from a state of illness/brokenness to wholeness, with the resulting state (being whole) persisting at the reference time, as evidenced by the directive 'go in peace' which presupposes the healing is complete and effective.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has made' describes a discrete bounded event (a transition into a new state of wholeness) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion about transitions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has made (you) whole' denotes a transition (from not-whole to whole) construed as a single completed bounded event, presenting the situation as a finished whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has made (you) whole' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world within the speaker's declaration, not projected as hypothetical, conditional, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has made' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'made' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'your faith' is the semantic agent (doer) and 'you' is the grammatical object (semantic patient); this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence).

FullText: Her parents were amazed . He charged them to tell no man what had been done .
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' introduces an overt internal argument (the implied patient/theme of the action), and passive voice instantiates ASTrns according to the positive examples provided.
ASUncc_explanation: The target verb 'had been done' is morphologically passive and licenses an external argument (the implied agent performing the action), which violates the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' (past participle of 'do') licenses an implicit internal argument (the action/thing that was done), making it transitive; additionally, in passive voice the sole expressed argument functions as a theme/patient rather than an external agent.
AktAcc_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a punctual change-of-state or completed action without a durative process phase inherent to the verb itself; it marks a boundary transition into a result state rather than an unfolding process leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The past perfect 'had been done' describes a completed action without specifying a punctual transition into a new result state; it refers to an unspecified prior event that unfolded as a process rather than marking a single temporal boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (something was accomplished/finished), lacking the dynamic durative unfolding required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The perfect aspect 'had been done' construes the event as a completed action with a resulting state (something was accomplished/performed), entailing a transition into a new state, which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('had been'), which entails dynamic unfolding and internal eventive progression, making it incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' presents a situation that actually occurred (he performed an action), using past perfect for temporal anteriority within narrative, without any counterfactual marking or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'no' applies to 'man' (negative subject of the infinitive clause), not to the target gram 'had been done', which describes a positive past event that occurred.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had been done' entails a change of state (something was accomplished/completed) and that resulting state is relevant to the discourse context (the parents are charged not to tell about what was done), making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a stative copula construction ('had been done') describing a completed state rather than encoding a transition; it reports a past condition without a persisting resulting state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (a miraculous healing or action) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the transition/result of that event.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect passive 'had been done' presents a bounded transition (the completion of an action) as a whole without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' reports a realized action in the actual world (the miraculous healing that occurred), embedded within a command about that actual event, not projected as hypothetical or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is a past perfect progressive form (past tense 'had' + past participle 'been' + past participle 'done'), which encodes tense specification other than simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' exhibits overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'had' + 'been' + past participle 'done'), with the semantic patient functioning as the grammatical subject of the relative clause, and no agent expressed.

FullText: Others said that Elijah had appeared and others and that one of the old prophets had risen again .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had appeared' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it only takes an external argument (Elijah) and describes a state or event without affecting a patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'appeared' encodes a one-participant event of coming-into-being/appearance with no external argument licensed, matching the positive example 'She has appeared.'
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'appeared' denotes an appearance/coming-into-being event where the sole argument (Elijah) functions as an internal argument (theme) rather than an external argument, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for existence/appearance verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'appear' denotes a punctual boundary transition into visibility or presence without a durative process phase, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had appeared' denotes a punctual transition into a perceptual or cognitive state (becoming visible/manifest), representing an instantaneous boundary event rather than an unfolding process, consistent with achievement semantics.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'appeared' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing/perceiving that Elijah appeared), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had appeared' entails a transition into a resulting state (Elijah being present/visible), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had appeared' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness (someone becoming visible), which involves dynamic change and eventive completion rather than a static condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had appeared' is embedded in reported speech ('Others said that...') and uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative context, not to indicate a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.

FullText: Others said that Elijah had appeared and others and that one of the old prophets had risen again .

AktAch_explanation: The event 'had risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (from dead to alive/resurrected), representing a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had risen' entails a change of location/state (from dead to alive), and that resulting state (being alive again) holds at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had risen' encodes a change of state from dead to alive (resurrection), with the resulting state of being alive persisting at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had risen' describes a discrete bounded event (a transition from dead to alive) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion about transitions into new states.

FullText: When the apostles returned they told Jesus about the things they had done . They traveled to the city of Bethsaida .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do' licenses an external argument (the apostles as agent/initiator), violating the requirement that the sole argument function as an internal argument without an external argument.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had done' refers to a range of unspecified activities ('things they had done') that unfold over measurable duration rather than a single punctual boundary transition, and it can naturally be construed as the completion of durative processes.

DtExp_explanation: The clause 'they had done' presents the apostles' actions as part of their life history without asserting that a resulting state currently holds; it answers whether the event occurred, and the subsequent sentence shifts to a new narrative event rather than describing a persistent result state.
DtRes_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' encodes a transition to a completed state (the apostles' actions are finished) that holds at the reference time of the narrative, entailing a resulting state of completion prior to their return.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'they had done' describes discrete bounded events (the apostles' activities during their mission) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on completed actions rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' presents the apostles' actions as a bounded whole completed prior to the reference time (their return), without profiling internal temporal development, instantiating the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'they had done' is embedded in a reported speech context ('told Jesus about the things they had done') and asserts a realized fact about what the apostles actually accomplished, not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had done' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes past tense specification other than simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'they' corresponds to the semantic agent (doer) of 'done', and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' auxiliary); this is active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: They said : » John the Baptist . Others say Elijah and still others say one of the old prophets has risen again . «

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has risen' entails a change of location/state (from dead to alive/present), and that resulting state holds at the reference time, making it non-experiential.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'one of the old prophets has risen again' is embedded within reported speech ('they said') and expresses what others believe or say rather than what is asserted as realized fact by the narrator, placing it in a domain of reported opinion rather than direct assertion of actuality.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'risen' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
