ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever sat on him' presents a factual situation (that no one has actually sat on the colt) as a statement of fact in the actual world, without any indication of counterfactuality or contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has sat' is directly negated by 'No man has ever sat on him', where the negator 'No' applies to the subject and the eventuality of sitting is presented as not occurring.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'No man has ever sat on him' presents the event as part of the colt's life history (whether anyone has ever ridden it), with the frequency adverbial 'ever' emphasizing the experiential reading rather than asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever sat on him' uses a frequency adverbial ('ever') and reports a past experiential state without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; the colt remains untouched, but this is not a change of state encoded by the verb.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'No man has ever sat on him' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of sitting) that has not occurred, rather than a continuous state or activity holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'sat' denotes a stative situation (being in a seated position) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not introduce a transition into or out of that state, making it unbounded rather than perfective.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'No man has ever sat on him' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (that no one has sat on the colt), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has sat' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'sat' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has sat' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'No man' as the semantic agent (the one performing the sitting action), not passive voice which requires 'have been' + past participle morphology.

FullText: He was near the descent of the Mount of Olives . The entire crowd of disciples rejoiced and praised God with a loud voice for all the mighty works they had seen .

DtRes_explanation: The clause 'for all the mighty works they had seen' reports a past perception event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it merely describes what the disciples had witnessed, not a change of state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'they had seen' describes discrete bounded events (the mighty works) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for 'one or more discrete bounded events.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had seen' presents the mighty works as bounded, completed events that the disciples witnessed, construing the perception as a discrete whole rather than profiling internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'they had seen' asserts a realized fact in the actual world—the disciples had actually witnessed the mighty works—and is not projected as hypothetical, conditional, future, or otherwise non-actual.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have made it a den of robbers' encodes a change of state from 'house of prayer' to 'den of robbers,' with the resulting state (the house currently being a den of robbers) persisting at the reference time of the utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'have made it a den of robbers' describes a discrete bounded event (a transformation/transition into a new state) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, matching the negative example 'I have eaten the rice (and now it is gone).'

FullText: Some of the scribes answered , » Teacher you have said well . «

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have said' reports a past utterance without entailing a persisting resulting state; it is a communication verb that merely describes a past interaction rather than one where the statement remains currently binding or in effect.

FullText: He said to them : » I have earnestly wanted to eat this Passover with you before I die .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'wanted' introduces an overt internal argument (the infinitival complement 'to eat this Passover with you'), which represents the affected theme of the wanting event directed from the external argument 'I' toward this internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'wanted' licenses an external argument (the subject 'I' as agent/experiencer) and introduces an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'to eat this Passover'), making it a two-participant event that does not meet the ASUncc criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'wanted' licenses an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'to eat this Passover with you'), which violates the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have wanted' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous entry into a mental state of desire without a durative process phase leading to a defined terminal point; wanting itself is a stative condition rather than an accomplishment with inherent endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have wanted' unfolds as a durative state of desire over a measurable duration rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and wanting does not entail a transition into a new result state but rather describes an ongoing emotional state.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'have earnestly wanted' denotes a cognitive state of desire without dynamic unfolding or sustained engagement; it lacks the dynamic, durative character required for AktAct and does not involve continuous action.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have wanted' unfolds over a measurable duration (from past to present) rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, and it does not meet the criteria for a semelfactive as it lacks the instantaneous, bounded nature required.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have (earnestly) wanted' expresses a durative mental state (desire/wanting) that has persisted over time without dynamic change or internal eventive unfolding, matching the positive example 'I have known him for ten years.'
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual statement of the speaker's actual desire/intention in the actual world, not a situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have (earnestly) wanted' is not negated; the negation would need to directly negate this verb phrase, but the sentence contains no negator applying to it.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'have earnestly wanted' presents a desire as part of the subject's life history without asserting a resulting state that currently holds; the wanting itself is the experiential entry, not a change of state or possession.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have wanted' expresses a psychological state or desire that has persisted over time, not a change of state with a resulting condition currently in effect; it describes an ongoing mental state rather than a transition to a new state.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have earnestly wanted' expresses a state of desire that has held continuously from a past point up to the reference time (the moment of speaking), fitting the definition of a situation holding across a time span rather than a discrete bounded event.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'wanted' denotes a stative situation (a mental state of desire) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not introduce a transition into or out of that state, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for stative situations.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have (earnestly) wanted' asserts a realized emotional state in the actual world (the speaker's past desire), not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle 'wanted' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have wanted' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent (the one doing the wanting); there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence) and no passive auxiliary structure.

FullText: He received a cup , and when he had given thanks , he said : » Take this , and share it among yourselves .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'give' introduces an overt internal argument 'thanks' (direct object), encoding a two-participant event directed from the external argument (he) toward the internal argument (thanks).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'give' in 'had given thanks' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he' as agent/initiator) and introduces an internal argument ('thanks' as direct object), violating the ASUncc feature requirements.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'give thanks' licenses an internal argument (the object 'thanks'), violating the requirement for zero internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'give thanks' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous speech act of expressing gratitude without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had given thanks' is a punctual transition into a new state (the completion of the act of giving thanks), marked by the temporal boundary 'when' and followed by a subsequent action, meeting the achievement criterion of a near-instantaneous change-of-state event.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had given thanks' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the completion of the act of giving thanks) and represents a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of a completed action, not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had given thanks' is construed as a single bounded occurrence (punctual action) without entailing a resulting change of state, fitting the semelfactive definition.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had given thanks' denotes a bounded dynamic event with a clear endpoint (the completion of the act of giving thanks), involving eventive change rather than a static condition that holds over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence ('when he had given thanks, he said'), indicating an event that actually occurred before another past event, without any counterfactual meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had given thanks' is not negated; no negator applies to this eventuality, which is presented as a realized event in the narrative sequence.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'when he had given thanks' presents the act of giving thanks as a completed event in the subject's life history that sets the temporal frame for the subsequent action ('he said'), without asserting that a resulting state of gratitude persists at the reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'give thanks' is a communication/performative act that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it reports a past interaction (the giving of thanks occurred) without a currently binding or persisting effect, similar to the negative example 'He has given me advice before.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the act of giving thanks) that occurred at a specific point in time, not a continuous situation holding across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had given thanks' presents the act of giving thanks as a bounded, completed transition event that occurred prior to the main clause action, fitting the perfective aspect definition of presenting a situation as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'when he had given thanks' asserts a realized event in the actual world as part of the narrative sequence of events, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had given thanks' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes past tense specification other than the simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'he' is the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of giving thanks; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence), and the construction is active voice.

FullText: » The Son of man indeed goes , as it has been determined . But woe to that man through whom he is betrayed ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'has been determined' introduces an overt internal argument (the implicit subject 'it' functioning as the affected patient), meeting the criterion for ASTrns as demonstrated in the positive examples for passive voice.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been determined'), and the lexical verb 'determine' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent performing the determination), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'determined' licenses an internal argument (the implicit theme 'it' referring to the Son of man's going), making it transitive and thus not a one-participant external-argument-only event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has been determined' denotes a punctual decision or establishment of a fixed outcome without a durative process phase; it marks a boundary transition into a determined state rather than an event unfolding over measurable duration toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'has been determined' denotes a punctual transition into a determined state—a single boundary point where something becomes fixed or decided—rather than an unfolding process, matching the achievement criterion of instantaneous change-of-state.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'has been determined' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the completion of a determination process with a defined result state (that something is now determined), lacking the dynamic durative unfolding required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been determined' entails a resulting state (something is now in a determined condition) and does not represent a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has been determined' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('has been'), which entails dynamic unfolding and internal eventive development incompatible with stative readings per CRI set_to_0_if rule 2.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'as it has been determined' presents a situation that has actually occurred (a past determination), not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been determined' is not negated; the negation 'not' in the sentence applies to the embedded infinitive 'to go' (implied in 'goes'), not to the target gram itself.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has been determined' entails a change of state (a decision/determination coming into effect) that holds at the reference time, making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'as it has been determined' uses a stative copula construction ('has been') with a past participle functioning as an adjective describing a state, not encoding a transition to a resulting state that persists at reference time; it merely reports that a determination was made in the past without entailing a persisting effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has been determined' describes a completed transition into a determined state (a punctual result of determination) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative example pattern of transition-focused states.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive perfect 'has been determined' presents a completed transition (the determination of the Son of man's path) as a bounded whole event, matching the positive example 'We have been discharged' and instantiating TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has been determined' asserts a realized fact in the actual world—that something has already been determined—without modal, conditional, future, or wish contexts that would project it as non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been determined' contains present-tense 'has' + past participle 'determined' with progressive marking ('been'), which constitutes additional marking beyond the simple present perfect and thus instantiates TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been determined' exhibits overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'has been' + past participle 'determined'), with the semantic patient functioning as the grammatical subject (the implicit referent of the clause), and the agent omitted.

FullText: » You have continued with me in my temptations .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have continued' introduces an overt internal argument ('with me in my temptations'), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant event directed from the external argument (You) toward an affected internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'continued' licenses an external argument (the subject 'You' as agent/initiator) and takes a prepositional object ('with me in my temptations'), violating the requirement for a one-participant internal-argument-only event.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'continued' licenses an internal argument ('with me in my temptations'), making it transitive and thus not a one-participant external-argument-only event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'continue' denotes an ongoing state or durative situation without an inherent endpoint or result state, and naturally accepts 'for X time' modification ('continued for hours'), which per CRI set_to_0_if criterion 3 indicates achievement rather than accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have continued' denotes an ongoing or durative process of remaining with someone through temptations, not a punctual transition into a new result state, and naturally accepts 'finish' as a complement ('finish continuing'), which disqualifies it as an achievement.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails a specific, inherent endpoint or defined result state—'have continued with me' marks the completion of a bounded period of accompaniment, and the perfect aspect indicates a finished state rather than an ongoing dynamic activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have continued' unfolds over a measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, and the perfect aspect indicates an ongoing state resulting from past action, both disqualifying it as semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have continued' denotes dynamic progression and internal eventive unfolding (the act of continuing involves ongoing change and development), which violates the stative requirement of holding without dynamic change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'have continued' as an actual occurrence in the real world without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have continued' is not negated; the sentence is affirmative and presents the event as occurring in reality.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'continued' entails an ongoing state of remaining/staying with the subject through temptations, which persists at the reference time, making this a result state rather than a life-history experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'continue' denotes an ongoing activity or state rather than a transition to a new resulting state; it describes persistence in a situation without entailing a change of state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have continued with me in my temptations' expresses a state or activity (remaining/staying with someone through temptations) that has held continuously from a past starting point to the reference time, matching the intuition for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'continue' denotes a stative situation of ongoing accompaniment without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not introduce a discrete bounded event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The present perfect 'have continued' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world as a fact, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish contexts present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have continued' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'continued', with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'You' is the semantic agent (doer) of the action 'continued', and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); this is active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: » I prayed for you that your faith would not fail . Once you have turned again strengthen your brothers . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have turned' is used without an overt internal argument in an activity reading ('Once you have turned again'), where no affected patient is syntactically realized, matching the set_to_0_if criterion.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'turned' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'you' as agent performing the action of turning/repenting), which violates the ASUncc feature requirement for a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'turned' licenses an internal argument (the reflexive/implicit object 'again' indicates directional change), and denotes a change-of-state event where the sole argument functions as a theme/patient rather than an external agent.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have turned' denotes a punctual change-of-state (a boundary transition from not-turned to turned) without a durative process phase, similar to 'arrive' or 'decide'.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have turned again' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (turning back/repenting) that crosses a definable threshold, regardless of any preceding process of spiritual struggle, matching the achievement pattern of 'reach' or 'arrive'.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'have turned again' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the completion of turning/repentance) and represents a bounded event marked by onset of a result state, not a dynamic durative activity without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have turned' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from spiritual failure to restored faith/strength), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have turned' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (turning from one condition to another) involving dynamic progression, which is incompatible with the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Once you have turned again' presents a situation as a future possibility within an open conditional structure ('Once you have turned...strengthen your brothers'), not as contrary to fact, so it does not instantiate ClCntfcl.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have turned' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('would not fail') applies to 'fail', not to the target gram.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have turned' entails a change of state/location (turning from faith-failure back to faith), and the resulting state of having turned is relevant for the subsequent action ('strengthen your brothers'), making this narrative progression non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'Once you have turned again' encodes a change of state (from not having turned to having turned) with the resulting state (being turned/converted) persisting at the reference time, which is the condition for the subsequent action of strengthening brothers.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'Once you have turned again' describes a discrete bounded event (the turning/conversion itself) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the transition into a new state of faith rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have turned' presents the transition of turning/returning as a bounded, completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect construal.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'Once you have turned again' is embedded within a conditional/temporal projection structure ('Once...then strengthen') that construes the turning as a future hypothetical event to be realized, not as an asserted actual fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have turned' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'turned' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have turned' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'you' as the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of turning; there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle sequence) and no agent expressed in a by-phrase.

FullText: They responded : » What further need do we have for witnesses ? We have heard this from his mouth . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' introduces an overt internal argument ('this') that is syntactically realized as a direct object, satisfying the requirement for a two-participant event with an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the subject 'we' as the experiencer/agent of the hearing event) and takes a direct object ('this'), violating both the external argument and overt object conditions for ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument ('this from his mouth'), violating the requirement for zero internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'heard' denotes a punctual perceptual event—a near-instantaneous transition into awareness upon hearing speech—without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (hearing the statement directly from the speaker's mouth), which is a single boundary event rather than an unfolding durative process, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news.'
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (hearing the blasphemy from his mouth), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic durative situation.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a resulting state of knowledge/perception and unfolds over a duration (the act of hearing something requires time), rather than occurring as a single punctual instantaneous occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness (hearing/perceiving the testimony), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if criterion.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'We have heard this from his mouth' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (they did hear it), with no indication of counterfactuality or unfulfilled conditions.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have heard' is not negated; the sentence is positive and asserts that they have heard something from his mouth.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have heard' entails perception of speech content, and the resulting state (knowledge of what was said) is relevant to the discourse context where witnesses question the need for further testimony based on what they have already heard.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' reports a past perceptual event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; hearing something does not create an enduring change of state like breaking or receiving does.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (hearing testimony directly from someone's mouth) rather than a continuous situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have heard' presents the auditory event as a bounded, completed whole without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'We have heard this from his mouth' asserts a realized fact in the actual world—a completed action with present relevance—not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'heard' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have heard' is active voice where the grammatical subject 'We' is the semantic agent (doer) and 'this' is the object; passive voice requires the morphological sequence 'have been + past participle', which is absent here.

FullText: Herod was very happy to see Jesus . He had wanted to see him for a long time . He hoped to see him perform a miracle .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had wanted' introduces an overt internal argument 'to see him' (infinitival complement functioning as the affected theme/patient of the wanting event), satisfying the two-participant transitive structure.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'wanted' licenses an external argument (the subject 'He' as agent/experiencer) and introduces an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'to see him'), making it a two-participant event that does not meet the ASUncc criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'wanted' licenses an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'to see him'), which violates the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'wanted' denotes a punctual mental state or attitude (desire) that came into being at a specific point in the past, lacking a durative process phase that culminates in a defined terminal point; the 'for a long time' modifies the duration of the state itself, not a process leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had wanted' is a durative state of desire extending over time ('for a long time'), not a punctual transition into a new result state, and fits the pattern of completion of a durative process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'wanted' denotes a stative mental state lacking dynamic unfolding and sustained engagement; it does not describe a dynamic, durative situation that unfolds over time.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had wanted' unfolds over a measurable duration ('for a long time') rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, making it a durative state rather than a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had wanted' is morphologically marked as past perfect progressive aspect (had + past participle), which entails dynamic unfolding and internal eventive development of a desire over time, incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He had wanted to see him for a long time' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (Herod's desire preceded the meeting), describing a factual state that actually occurred, without any indication of counterfactuality.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had wanted' is not negated; the sentence expresses a positive desire that occurred in the past, with no negator applying to this eventuality.
DtExp_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'for a long time', indicating a continuing situation rather than a completed life experience, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'for a long time', which indicates a continuing situation rather than a completed transition to a resulting state, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'had wanted to see him for a long time' expresses a state of desire that held continuously from a past point up to the reference time, matching the intuition of a situation holding across a time span with the duration marker 'for a long time'.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'He had wanted to see him for a long time' contains the duration adverbial 'for a long time' which makes the internal temporal span accessible rather than packaging the situation as a bounded whole, and 'wanted' is a stative predicate without a construed boundary or transition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had wanted' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (Herod's past desire as a fact), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'had wanted' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes past tense specification other than the simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'He' corresponds to the semantic agent (the one doing the wanting), and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' + past participle); this is active voice.

FullText: He said : » You brought this man to me as one who perverted the people . I examined him before you and find no fault in this man . He has not done the things you accuse him of .
Target: has not done (the things you accuse him of)
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'done' introduces an overt internal argument 'the things you accuse him of', establishing a two-participant transitive event directed from the external argument (he) toward the internal argument (the things).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he' as agent) and takes an implicit direct object (the things accused), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('the things you accuse him of'), which is a direct object, thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'do' in this context denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous change-of-state (committing an act) without a durative process phase; the negation emphasizes the absence of a boundary event rather than a durative accomplishment with an inherent endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'do the things you accuse him of' is durative and process-oriented rather than a punctual state-change transition; it unfolds over measurable duration and does not represent crossing a definable threshold into a new result state.
AktAct_explanation: The situation entails a specific, defined result state (the non-occurrence of particular accusatory actions), and the verb phrase denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the completion of an examination and judgment rather than a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has not done (the things you accuse him of)' is a negated perfective construction that does not instantiate a single punctual event; rather, it denies the completion of actions that would unfold over time and entail resulting states, making it a non-semelfactive aspect.
AktState_explanation: The perfect aspect 'has not done' denotes a bounded dynamic event (the performance or non-performance of actions) with an inherent endpoint, involving eventive change rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual assertion about the actual world ('He has not done the things you accuse him of') based on an examination that occurred, without any indication of counterfactuality or contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has not done' contains direct syntactic negation ('not') that negates the eventuality of doing the things accused, presenting the event as not occurring.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'has not done' presents the non-occurrence of the accused actions as an entry in the subject's life history without asserting any resulting state; it answers whether the event occurred at least once, negatively.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is negated ('has not done'), which entails that no transition to a resulting state occurs; the set_to_0_if condition 'The clause negates the occurrence of the event; no transition to a resulting state is entailed' applies.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes the absence of discrete bounded events (the accused things) rather than a continuous state or activity holding over time, focusing on what has not occurred rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'has not done' is negated with 'not', indicating the transition has not occurred and the situation remains open, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'He has not done the things you accuse him of' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world as a factual conclusion from examination, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has not done' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'done' with no additional modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form specified in set_to_0_if.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has not done' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'He' as the semantic agent (doer); it lacks the passive morphology of 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » Herod found no fault and sent him back to us . He has done nothing worthy of death .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has done' introduces an overt internal argument 'nothing' (direct object), instantiating a two-participant event with an external argument (He) and an affected internal argument (nothing).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'he') and takes an internal argument ('nothing'), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the one-participant internal-argument-only requirement.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument 'nothing' (direct object), violating the requirement for zero internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'do nothing' denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous state of non-action without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint, fitting the set_to_0_if criterion for boundary transitions and instantaneous changes.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'has done nothing' describes the absence of action over an unspecified duration rather than a punctual transition into a new result state, and does not represent crossing a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'has done nothing' lacks dynamic unfolding and sustained engagement; it denotes a state of non-action (absence of accomplishment) rather than a durative activity unfolding over time.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'has done nothing' is a stative/negative construction describing a state of non-action rather than a dynamic punctual event, and does not meet the criteria for a semelfactive (single bounded occurrence without change of state).
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has done nothing' is morphologically marked as present perfect progressive aspect ('has done') denoting a completed dynamic action/event, which involves eventive change and is incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents the situation as actually occurring in the actual world (Herod's factual assessment that he found no fault), without any indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'nothing' modifies 'worthy of death' (the predicate complement), not the eventuality expressed by 'has done'; the target gram asserts that Herod performed an action (doing something), which is realized rather than negated.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'He has done nothing worthy of death' presents a negative state of affairs (absence of wrongdoing) that is relevant to the current judgment and justifies sending him back, making it a current state rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'do nothing' is an activity/non-action that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; it merely reports a past situation without encoding a transition to a new state that holds currently.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a state of non-action (nothing worthy of death) as a completed assessment rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the absence of a culpable act rather than duration.

FullText: A third time he said to them : » Why , what evil has this man done ? I have found no reason to put him to death . I will therefore chastise him and release him . «

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has done (evil)' entails a resulting state of having committed wrongdoing and is construed as a durative accomplishment rather than a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.

DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has done (evil)' entails a change of state (the man's culpability or guilt status), and this resulting state is relevant to the subsequent discourse where the speaker justifies releasing him, making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has done' reports a past action (committing evil) without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it is a simple past interaction query, similar to the negative example 'He has given me advice before.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the man committing evil) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the action itself rather than its duration.

VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'this man' corresponds to the semantic agent (doer) of 'done', and there is no passive morphology (no 'been' auxiliary); this is active voice.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram asserts 'has done nothing wrong' as a realized fact about the man in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context; negation alone does not trigger irrealis.

FullText: It was early in the morning on the first day of the week . They went to the tomb with the spices that they had prepared .

AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had prepared' denotes a durative process of arranging and readying spices that culminates in a defined result state (spices ready for use), fitting the accomplishment profile of AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'had prepared' unfolds over a measurable duration involving multiple steps (gathering, arranging spices) rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and it can naturally be framed as completion of a durative process.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had prepared' entails a resulting change of state (spices transition from unprepared to prepared) and describes a completed action with a consequent result condition, not a punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had prepared' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving internal development and change-of-state (from unprepared to prepared state), which entails dynamic progression incompatible with AktState.

DtRes_explanation: The past perfect 'had prepared' encodes a transition from unprepared to prepared spices, with the resulting state (spices being ready/prepared) persisting at the reference time when they went to the tomb.
DtUniv_explanation: The past perfect 'had prepared' describes a discrete, bounded event (the preparation of spices) completed before the main action, not a continuous situation holding over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had prepared' presents the preparation of spices as a completed, bounded event prior to the main action, construing the transition from unprepared to prepared state as a single whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had prepared' asserts a realized past action within the narrative of actual events; it describes a completed fact (the spices were prepared before they went to the tomb) and is not projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.

FullText: They were perplexed about what had happened . Two men stood by them in dazzling apparel .

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had happened' is a past perfect construction referring to an unspecified prior event, construed as durative or with resulting state change rather than a single punctual occurrence without consequence.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had happened' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event with an inherent endpoint, involving change and eventive unfolding rather than a static condition that holds uniformly over time.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had happened' is not negated; the sentence expresses that an event did occur, and the negation (implicit in 'perplexed') applies to understanding/knowledge rather than to the eventuality itself.

DtRes_explanation: The clause 'what had happened' is embedded in a relative construction reporting a past event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it merely describes a prior occurrence that caused perplexity, not a transition with a current effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (something that happened) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the event itself rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had happened' presents a transition event (something occurring) as a bounded, completed whole without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect definition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The clause 'what had happened' is embedded as the object of 'were perplexed about,' asserting a realized past event in the actual world rather than projecting a hypothetical, conditional, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had happened' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes tense specification other than simple present perfect, satisfying the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had happened' is an active voice construction where the grammatical subject 'what' is the semantic agent/experiencer of the intransitive verb 'happen'; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle, which is absent here.

FullText: The one named Cleopas answered him : » Do you travel alone in Jerusalem and not know the things that have happened there these days ? «

FullText: » They did not find his body . They said that they had also seen a vision of angels , who said that he was alive .

FullText: When he had sat down with them to eat he took the bread and blessed ; and breaking it he gave to them .

FullText: » The Lord has risen indeed , « they said , » and he appeared to Simon . «

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has seen' with the frequency adverbial 'ever' reports a past experiential event without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; no man currently possesses a state of having-seen-God as a change of condition.

DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes discrete bounded events (seeing and bearing witness as completed actions) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and the focus is on the accomplished acts themselves rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have borne witness' presents the act of bearing witness as a bounded, completed transition event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect criterion.

TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have found' presents the transition of discovering the Messiah as a bounded, completed event without profiling its internal temporal development, matching the perfective aspect criterion.

FullText: He said to him : » Every man sets out the good wine first . When the men have drunk freely the lower quality wine is served . You have kept the good wine until now . «
